title: प्रथमोनुवाकः — Word Dictionary
source: Claude analysis of pada-pāṭha word splitting with references from Nirukta, Nighantu, Pāṇini Aṣṭādhyāyī, Amara Kosha, and Vedāntic commentaries
structure: Global dictionary of unique base words (lemmas), followed by per-mantra word sequences with pada positions referencing the dictionary

panini: √नम् + असुन् (Uṇādi 4.188) → नमस् — the asun-pratyaya forms the abstract noun
P 8.3.34 — visarjanīya (ḥ) at pada-end before pause (avasāna)
P 8.3.15 — visarjanīya rules for s → ḥ in sentence-final position

meanings.english: Salutation; obeisance; reverential bowing; surrender; homage
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.18 — listed among karmavācaka words (action-denoting terms). Naighaṇṭuka-kāṇḍa groups it with acts of reverence and worship.
meanings.nirukta: Yāska (Nirukta 3.16 and 10.3) derives from √nam — 'namati praṇamati' (one bows down, prostrates). The visarga form namaḥ indicates the completed act of submission. Some nairuktikas parse as 'na + maḥ' — 'not mine', indicating relinquishment of ego, though Yāska himself favors the verbal derivation.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.3.213 — नमः listed as an avyaya (indeclinable) meaning 'praṇāma' (prostration). Amara classifies it among common liturgical expressions.
meanings.vedantic: In Advaita, 'namaḥ' is parsed as 'na mama' (not mine) — a surrender of kartṛtva and bhoktṛtva (doership and enjoyership). Śaṅkara in his bhāṣya treats namaḥ as complete effacement of ahaṅkāra before the Supreme. In Śaiva Siddhānta, it represents prapatti (total surrender) to Rudra-Śiva. The Śvetāśvatara Upaniṣad (4.22) uses 'namaḥ' in the same spirit when addressing Rudra.

grammar.form: enclitic dative singular 'tubhyam' or genitive singular 'tava'
panini: P 8.1.22 — te, me, etc. are treated as enclitic (anudātta) forms that cannot begin a sentence (pragraha rules)
P 8.1.28 — te is an ādeśa (replacement) of tubhyam/tava in non-initial positions
P 7.2.97 — the dative singular replacement for yuṣmad-śabda

meanings.english: To you; your; of you; for you
meanings.nighantu: Not separately listed; pronouns are treated as structural words (nipāta-varga) in the Nighantu tradition.
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 1.4 discusses pronoun forms. The enclitic 'te' is the natural conversational form of address in Vedic, more intimate than the full 'tubhyam' — it implies direct, personal communion with the deity.
meanings.vedantic: Here 'te' addresses Rudra as the Supreme Being directly. The repeated use throughout Śrī Rudram establishes the devotee's continuous awareness of the divine 'You' — the eternal subject who is never an object. In Vedāntic reading, 'te' points to the Ātman-Brahman that is the true 'You' of all beings (cf. 'tat tvam asi').

rudra.root: √rud (to cry, to howl) + ra (agent suffix); or √ru (to break, to dissolve) + dra (one who drives/runs)

grammar.form: vocative singular, masculine (also nom. sg. in other contexts)
panini: √रुद् + रक् (Uṇādi 2.22) → रुद्र — agent noun formation
P 2.4.62 — Rudra as a proper noun (saṃjñā-śabda) retains its form in compounds
P 1.2.38 — as a name of a deity, it follows devata-specific accent rules

meanings.english: The Howler; the Terrible; the Remover of sorrow; the one who drives away suffering; the Red/Shining one
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 5.3 (Daivata-kāṇḍa) — listed among the names of deities of the middle region (antarikṣa-sthāna-devatā). Also appears in the enumeration of Ādityas and in storm-deity lists.
meanings.nirukta: Yāska (Nirukta 10.3) gives multiple etymologies: (1) 'rodayati iti rudraḥ' — He who makes enemies weep; (2) 'rut dravayati iti rudraḥ' — He who drives away suffering (rut = duḥkha, dravayati = causes to flow away); (3) 'raudīti yaḥ sa rudraḥ' — He who roars/howls. The Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa (6.1.3.7) derives from 'arudat' — the crying of Prajāpati's offspring who demanded a name. Nirukta also notes the interpretation 'rut + dra' where rut = disease/sorrow.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.29 (Svargavarga, Śiva-nāma) — रुद्रः listed among the principal names of Śiva: 'śambhurīśaḥ paśupatiḥ śivaḥ śūlī maheśvaraḥ / īśvaraḥ sarva īśānaḥ śaṅkaraś candraśekharaḥ' — Rudra is one of the 27 synonyms of Śiva given by Amara.
meanings.vedantic: In Advaita, Rudra is Brahman in the aspect of cosmic dissolution (saṃhāra-mūrti). Sāyaṇa glosses Rudra as 'duḥkhaṃ rut tad drāvayati iti rudraḥ'. In Śrī Rudra Bhāṣya attributed to Śaṅkara, Rudra is identified with the Supreme Ātman. Abhinavagupta reads Rudra as primal consciousness that dissolves duality. The Śvetāśvatara Upaniṣad (3.2) declares 'eko hi rudro na dvitīyāya tasthur' — Rudra alone exists, there is no second.

manyu.root: √man (to think, to be incensed, to be passionate, to conceive)

grammar.form: appears as मन्यवे (manyave, dative sg.) in mantra 1
panini: √मन् + युच् (P 3.1.144) → मन्यु — the yuc-pratyaya forms action/result nouns
P 7.1.13 — dative singular of yu-anta words: मन्यु + ए → मन्यवे (y → v intervocalically)

meanings.english: Wrath; fierce passion; zealous spirit; high spirit; indignation; the force of divine will
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 2.3 — listed among krodha-vācaka words (anger-denoting terms). Also appears in Nighantu 1.8 among terms for mental states (manas-vṛtti).
meanings.nirukta: Yāska derives manyu from √man — 'manyate krodham iti manyuḥ' (that which is conceived as fury). Also connected to 'manas' — the intense activity of mind. Manyu is not mere anger but concentrated force of will — divine fury that upholds ṛta. Cf. RV 10.83-84 (Manyu Sūkta) where Manyu is itself invoked as a deity: 'yaste manyo'vidhad vajra sāyaka' — O Manyu, you whose bolt is unfailing.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.7.25 (Krodha-varga) — मन्युः listed as a synonym of krodha (anger): 'manyuḥ krodhāmarṣaṇau roṣakopau pratighāv ubhau' — Amara lists manyu alongside krodha, amarṣa, roṣa, kopa, and pratigha as words for anger.
meanings.vedantic: Sāyaṇa glosses manyave as 'krodhāya'. Vedāntic commentators note Rudra's manyu is not tāmasic anger but sāttvic fury that destroys adharma and ignorance — the same force Kṛṣṇa displays on the battlefield. The salutation to manyu acknowledges that even divine wrath serves a liberating purpose.

panini: P 1.1.37 — classified as a nipāta (particle), which by P 1.1.38 are sarvadā anudāttāḥ (always anudātta) except when specifically accented by Vedic usage
P 2.2.29 — uta as a samuccaya (cumulative conjunction) in dvandva-like constructions

meanings.english: And; also; moreover; as well as; further
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.29 — listed among samuccayārthaka avyayas (conjunctive particles). The Nighantu tradition recognizes its use in both additive and emphatic senses.
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 1.9 — Yāska classifies uta among the upamārthīya and samuccayārthīya particles. In doubled form 'utota' (uta+uta), it emphasizes cumulative addition — 'and further also'. The Nirukta notes that nipātas modify the meaning of adjacent words without independent denotation.
meanings.vedantic: The repeated 'uta' extends the salutation to every aspect of Rudra — not just to Rudra himself but also to his instruments. This reveals the Vedic insight that the divine pervades every aspect, even the weapons that cause fear.

iṣu.root: √iṣ (to send forth, to propel, to impel, to seek)

grammar.form: appears as इषवे (iṣave, dat. sg.), इषुः (iṣuḥ, nom. sg.), इषुम् (iṣum, acc. sg.), इषवः (iṣavaḥ, nom. pl.) across mantras
panini: √इष् + उ (Uṇādi 1.1) → इषु — the u-pratyaya forms the substantive noun
P 7.1.73 — declension follows u-kārānta (u-stem) paradigm: nom. इषुः, dat. इषवे, acc. इषुम्, nom.pl. इषवः
P 6.1.77 — sandhi rule for iṣu + e → iṣave

meanings.english: Arrow; missile; shaft; projectile
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 2.20 — listed among āyudha-nāmāni (weapon names) alongside śara, bāṇa, sāyaka.
meanings.nirukta: Yāska derives iṣu from √iṣ — 'iṣyate kṣipyate iti iṣuḥ' (that which is sent forth / hurled). The root √iṣ carries the dual sense of 'impelling/seeking' and 'desiring' — an arrow is that which is directed with intention toward its target. Nirukta 9.11 discusses arrow-related vocabulary.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.8.86 (Kṣatriya-varga, Āyudha) — इषुः listed as a synonym of arrow: 'iṣuḥ śaraś ca bāṇaś ca' — iṣu, śara, and bāṇa are synonyms for arrow.
meanings.vedantic: The arrow symbolizes Rudra's targeted grace or punishment — it never misses. In the Muṇḍaka Upaniṣad (2.2.4): 'śaram hy upāsā-niśitam sandhayīta' — the arrow (jīvātman) is sharpened by meditation and aimed at Brahman. Saluting the arrow acknowledges that even what strikes us is an instrument of divine purpose.

grammar.forms: अस्तु (astu, 3rd sg. imperative 'may it be'), असत् (asat, 3rd sg. optative/conditional 'would be')
panini: P 2.4.52 — √अस् is replaced by भू in certain lakaras (bhū-ādeśa)
P 3.4.86 — loṭ lakāra (imperative) formation → अस् + तु → अस्तु
P 7.1.35 — the tu-ending for 3rd person imperative
P 3.4.113 — liṅ lakāra (optative): अस् + ईत् → स्यात् / असत् (Vedic conditional)

meanings.english: May it be; let it be; let there be; would be; may it become
meanings.nirukta: The imperative of √as. Expresses a benedictory wish — 'let this salutation exist, let it come into being.' The imperative mood elevates the salutation from description to performative speech act.
meanings.vedantic: 'Astu' transforms the salutation into a prayer — not merely stating 'salutation to you' but earnestly invoking 'may my salutation reach you.' This reflects the Vedic understanding that mantra efficacy depends on sankalpa (intention). In mantra 5, 'asat' (optative 'may it be') expresses the wish for the world to be free of disease.

dhanvan.root: √धन् (to sound, to resonate) or √धन्व् (to move in a curve, to run)

grammar.forms: धन्वने (dhanvane, dat. sg.), धनुः (dhanuḥ, nom. sg.), धन्वनः (dhanvanaḥ, gen./abl. sg.)
panini: √धन् + वनिप् (Uṇādi 4.113) → धन्वन् — vanip-pratyaya forms the n-stem noun
P 7.1.75 — an-anta (n-stem) declension paradigm: nom. धनुः (with P 8.2.7 n → anusvāra → ḥ), dat. धन्वने
P 6.4.8 — sarvanāmasthāna rules for the alternation between dhanvan-/dhanus- stems
P 8.2.7 — conversion of final n to visarjanīya in nom. sg.: dhanvans → dhanuḥ

meanings.english: Bow; the arc-shaped weapon
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 2.20 — listed among āyudha-nāmāni (weapon names). The Naighaṇṭuka tradition pairs it with iṣu as the primary weapon-set.
meanings.nirukta: Yāska connects dhanvan to √dhan (to produce sound) — the bow is characterized by its resonant twang when released. Also related to √dhanv (to curve/run) — its arc shape. Nirukta 9.11 notes the relationship between the bow's curve and the concept of all-pervading reach.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.8.85 (Kṣatriya-varga, Āyudha) — 'dhanuḥ koḍaṇḍam' — dhanus and koḍaṇḍa are synonyms for bow. Also 'cāpaḥ śarāsanam' as further synonyms.
meanings.vedantic: Muṇḍaka Upaniṣad (2.2.4): 'dhanur gṛhītvā aupaniṣadaṃ mahāstram' — 'Taking up the great weapon, the bow of the Upaniṣads' — the bow is Oṃkāra (Praṇava). It is both the vehicle of destruction and liberation. In Śrī Rudram context, saluting the bow acknowledges the instrument through which divine will operates.

bāhu.root: √बाह् / √बंह् (to grow, to increase, to be strong, to extend)

grammar.forms: बाहुभ्याम् (bāhubhyām, dat./instr. dual)
panini: √बंह् + उ (Uṇādi 1.22) → बाहु — u-pratyaya with vṛddhi of root vowel
P 7.1.73 — u-kārānta (u-stem) declension: dual dat./instr. बाहुभ्याम्
P 7.3.113 — dual case ending -bhyām applies directly to u-stem

meanings.english: Arm; forearm; the upper limb from shoulder to hand
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 2.4 — listed among aṅga-nāmāni (body-part names) in the Naighaṇṭuka-kāṇḍa.
meanings.nirukta: Yāska connects bāhu to √baṃh — 'baṃhate vardhate iti bāhuḥ' (that which grows strong / extends). The dual form refers specifically to Rudra's two arms as distinct instruments of power.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.6.73 (Manuṣya-varga, Aṅga) — बाहुः listed among arm-names: 'bāhur dorbhujau' — bāhu, dos, and bhuja are synonyms for arm.
meanings.vedantic: The two arms represent Rudra's dual function — protection (abhaya) and discipline (daṇḍa). Saluting the arms separately from the weapons shows the Vedic seer's comprehensive vision — every limb of the divine merits reverence. The dual form hints at the pairs of opposites (dvandva) that Rudra transcends.

yad.root: यद् (yad — relative pronoun: who, which, what)

grammar.forms: या (yā, fem. nom. sg.), याम् (yām, fem. acc. sg.), यः (yaḥ, masc. nom. sg.), ये (ye, masc. nom. pl.), याः (yāḥ, fem. nom. pl.)
panini: P 1.1.27 — yad is classified as a sarvanāman (pronoun)
P 7.2.102-105 — declension paradigm for yad-śabda across genders
P 8.1.36 — ya-forms can be accent-bearing or enclitic depending on position

meanings.english: Who; which; what; that which (relative pronoun forming correlative clauses)
meanings.nirukta: Yāska classifies yad among relative-correlative constructions fundamental to Vedic syntax. The relative clause structure 'yā te iṣuḥ ... tayā naḥ mṛḍaya' (which arrow of yours ... by that, be gracious to us) is a defining pattern of Vedic prayer — naming the fearsome and asking for its transformation.
meanings.vedantic: The relative pronoun 'yā/yad' in Vedic prayer introduces the item of fear which the devotee then asks the deity to make auspicious. This rhetorical structure embodies surrender — not denying the terrible but asking it to become gracious.

śiva.root: √śī (to lie, to rest, to bring peace) + va (suffix); or from √śiv (to be auspicious)

grammar.forms: शिवतमा (śivatamā, fem. superl. nom. sg.), शिवम् (śivam, neut. nom/acc. sg.), शिवा (śivā, fem. nom. sg.), शिवाम् (śivām, fem. acc. sg.), शिवेन (śivena, masc./neut. instr. sg.), शिवः (śivaḥ, masc. nom. sg.)
panini: P 5.3.55 — tamac-pratyaya for superlative: शिव + तम → शिवतम (most auspicious), fem. शिवतमा
P 4.1.4 — fem. ā-ending for adjectives: śiva → śivā
P 2.4.62 — as a proper name (Śiva), retains form in compounds

meanings.english: Auspicious; benign; gracious; kind; peaceful; propitious; the one who brings well-being
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.19 — listed among kalyāṇa-vācaka words (auspiciousness-denoting terms). Also in Nighantu 5.3 among deity-epithets.
meanings.nirukta: Yāska derives śiva from √śī — 'śete sukham yasmin sa śivaḥ' (in whom/which one rests in comfort). Also parsed as 'śam + iva' — 'like peace itself'. The Nirukta tradition emphasizes that śiva is fundamentally a quality-word (guṇa-vācaka) before becoming a proper name — it describes the benign aspect of the same deity whose terrible form is 'Rudra'.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.29 — शिवः listed as one of the 27 names of the deity: 'śambhurīśaḥ paśupatiḥ śivaḥ'. Also Amarakośa 3.3.97 — śiva as an adjective meaning maṅgala (auspicious): 'śivaḥ śaṅkare cāpi kalyāṇe ca'.
meanings.vedantic: The transition from 'Rudra' to 'Śiva' is the central theological movement of the Śrī Rudram — the terrible is asked to become auspicious. In Advaita, Śiva represents the nirguṇa Brahman beyond attributes, whose nature is inherently śānta (peaceful). Rudra's weapons becoming 'śiva' symbolizes the transformation of worldly suffering into spiritual awakening.

bhū.root: √भू (to be, to become, to arise, to exist) — bhvādi, parasmaipada

grammar.forms: बभूव (babhūva, 3rd sg. perfect 'has become'), भव (bhava, 2nd sg. imperative 'be!'), भूतानि (bhūtāni, neut. nom. pl. 'beings')
panini: P 3.2.115 — liṭ lakāra (perfect): भू → ब + भू + व → बभूव (with reduplication per P 6.1.8)
P 3.4.87 — loṭ lakāra (imperative): भू + हि → भव (with guṇa per P 7.3.84)
P 3.2.107 — past participle: भू + क्त → भूत
P 3.3.18 — verbal noun formation: भू + क्तिन् → भूतिः (prosperity)

meanings.english: To become; to be; to arise; to exist; to happen
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 1.1 — √bhū listed among the most fundamental sattā-vācaka dhātus (existence-denoting roots).
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 2.1 — Yāska establishes √bhū as the root of all becoming. 'Bhavati' denotes coming-into-being, while 'babhūva' (perfect) indicates accomplished/established existence. The Nirukta treats bhū as the most productive root in the language.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.3.213 — भूतम् (past participle) listed meaning 'prāpta' (obtained) and 'jāta' (born). भूतानि as 'beings' appears in Amarakośa 1.1.7.
meanings.vedantic: In Vedānta, √bhū represents the principle of manifestation — Brahman becoming the world. 'Babhūva te dhanuḥ' — 'your bow has become [auspicious]' — represents the transformation of the terrible into the benign through prayer.

śaravyā.root: शर (śara — arrow) + व्यच् (vyac — suffix indicating container/holder), or शर + वय (that which bears arrows)

grammar.base: शरव्या (śaravyā — quiver; volley of arrows; that which relates to arrows)
panini: P 4.2.49 — yat-pratyaya for 'belonging to / relating to': शर + य → शरव्य, fem. शरव्या
P 4.1.4 — feminine ā-ending (ṭāp)

meanings.english: Quiver; arrow-case; the bundle/volley of arrows; that which pertains to arrows
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 2.20 — grouped with āyudha-nāmāni (weapon names).
meanings.nirukta: Derived from śara (arrow) — 'śarāṇām samūhaḥ / ādhāraḥ' (a collection of arrows / their container). Sāyaṇa glosses as 'bāṇa-samūha' (arrow-collection) or 'tūṇīra' (quiver).
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.8.86 — related terms: 'tūṇīraḥ iṣudhiḥ niṣaṅgaḥ' are listed as quiver-synonyms.
meanings.vedantic: The quiver holds potential arrows — representing latent destructive capacity. Asking for the quiver to be śivā means asking that even the reservoir of Rudra's power be turned to benevolence.

grammar.form: genitive singular — 'your, of you' (full/non-enclitic form, unlike 'te')
panini: P 8.1.22 — unlike 'te', 'tava' is the full (pragṛhya) genitive form that can begin a sentence
P 7.2.97 — genitive singular formation of yuṣmad-śabda

meanings.english: Your; of you; belonging to you
meanings.nirukta: The full genitive form 'tava' is used when emphasis or sentence-initial position requires the stressed pronoun form, as opposed to the enclitic 'te'.
meanings.vedantic: Same referent as 'te' — addresses Rudra directly. The full form 'tava' carries greater emphasis — 'yours specifically, O Rudra'.

grammar.forms: तया (tayā, fem. instr. sg. 'by that'), ताम् (tām, fem. acc. sg. 'that'), ताः (tāḥ, fem. nom/acc. pl.), तम् (tam, masc. acc. sg.), सः (saḥ, masc. nom. sg. — suppletion), तेभ्यः (tebhyaḥ, dat. pl.)
panini: P 1.1.27 — tad classified as sarvanāman
P 7.2.102 — declension paradigm for tad-śabda
P 7.1.25 — suppletion: nom. sg. masc. becomes saḥ (not tadaḥ)

meanings.english: That; by that; him/her/it (demonstrative/correlative pronoun)
meanings.nirukta: Functions as the correlative partner to yad (relative pronoun). The construction 'yā te iṣuḥ ... tayā naḥ mṛḍaya' uses yā...tayā as relative-correlative pair.
meanings.vedantic: In Vedāntic usage, 'tad' famously refers to Brahman — 'tat tvam asi' (Chāndogya 6.8.7). The correlative use here points to the identity between the terrible and the auspicious.

asmad.root: अस्मद् (asmad — first person pronoun: we, us)

grammar.forms: नः (naḥ, enclitic acc./dat./gen. 'us/to us/our'), अस्मान् (asmān, acc. pl. 'us'), अस्मत् (asmat, abl. 'from us')
panini: P 8.1.22 — नः is the enclitic form, governed by same pragraha rules as ते
P 7.2.97 — accusative plural: अस्मान्
P 7.2.86 — ablative: अस्मत्

meanings.english: Us; to us; our; from us (first person plural)
meanings.nirukta: The first person plural establishes the communal nature of Vedic prayer — the ṛṣi speaks not for himself alone but for the community.
meanings.vedantic: The shift from 'te' (you) to 'naḥ' (us) establishes the devotee-deity relationship. In Advaita, the apparent duality of 'you' and 'us' is ultimately resolved in the identity of Ātman and Brahman.

mṛḍ.root: √मृड् (to be gracious, to show mercy, to have compassion, to make happy) — tud-ādi or cur-ādi

grammar.forms: मृडय (mṛḍaya, 2nd sg. imperative causative 'be gracious! have mercy!'), मृडयाति (mṛḍayāti, 3rd sg. present causative 'he shows mercy')
panini: P 3.1.26 — causative (ṇic) formation: मृड् + णिच् → मृडय (with guṇa)
P 3.4.87 — loṭ (imperative): मृडय + हि → मृडय (hi dropped after aya per Vedic usage)

meanings.english: Be gracious; show mercy; have compassion; make happy; delight us
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.13 — listed among sukha-vācaka (happiness-denoting) roots.
meanings.nirukta: Yāska connects mṛḍ to the idea of 'softening' — 'mṛdnāti duḥkham' (grinds away suffering). Related to mṛdu (soft, gentle). The causative form mṛḍaya means 'cause to be happy/gentle' — a direct plea for Rudra to exercise his gracious nature.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.1.32 — मृडयति listed as a kriyā (verb) meaning 'sukhayati' (to make happy).
meanings.vedantic: Mṛḍaya is the central prayer of Śrī Rudram — asking Rudra to be gracious. In the Śaiva tradition, mṛḍa is itself an epithet of Śiva (Mṛḍa = the gracious one). The plea transforms the hymn from a litany of fearsome attributes into a loving petition.

tanū.root: √तन् (to stretch, to extend, to spread) + ऊ (suffix)

grammar.forms: तनूः (tanūḥ, nom. sg. 'body/form'), तनुवा (tanuvā, instr. sg. 'by/with that form')
panini: √तन् + ऊ (Uṇādi 1.67) → तनू
P 7.1.73 — ū-kārānta declension: nom. तनूः, instr. तनुवा (with v-insertion per P 6.1.79)

meanings.english: Body; form; self; person; embodiment
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 2.4 — listed among śarīra-vācaka (body-denoting) words and also among ātman-vācaka (self-denoting) words.
meanings.nirukta: Yāska derives from √tan — 'tanyate vistarayate iti tanuḥ' (that which is spread out/extended). The body is the 'extension' of the self into physical form. In Vedic usage, tanū often means 'self' (ātman) rather than just physical body.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.6.71 — तनूः listed in the śarīra-varga (body section): 'śarīraṃ vapuḥ tanuḥ' — body, form, and tanu are synonyms.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 3, 'yā te śivā tanūḥ' — 'which auspicious form of yours' — tanū refers to a particular aspect or manifestation of Rudra. The Vedāntic reading sees tanū as the 'extension' of the formless Brahman into the realm of name and form. The prayer asks for Rudra's benign form (śivā tanūḥ) to be the one that faces us.

aghorā.root: अ (not) + घोर (terrible, dreadful) — from √ghur / √ghuṣ (to sound terribly)

panini: P 6.2.155 — nañ-tatpuruṣa (negative compound): a + ghora → aghora
P 2.2.6 — nañ (negative particle) precedes the adjective in compound
P 4.1.4 — feminine ā-ending: aghorā

meanings.english: Not terrible; benign; gentle; free from horror; mild
meanings.nirukta: Compound of nañ (negative) + ghora (terrible). Ghora is derived from √ghuṣ/ghur — 'ghorayati bhāyayati iti ghoram' (that which causes fear). A-ghora is the deliberate negation of Rudra's terrifying aspect. The Nirukta tradition reads negation-compounds as more than simple absence — a-ghora implies active gentleness, not mere lack of terror.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.1.49 — घोरम् listed as a synonym of 'bhayānaka' (terrifying). Aghora is its antonym by nañ-samāsa.
meanings.vedantic: Aghorā is one of the Pañcabrahma-mantras (Taittirīya Āraṇyaka 10.43-47) — 'Aghora' faces south and represents the dissolution aspect that is simultaneously benign. In Kāśmīra Śaivism, Aghora is the face of Śiva that makes the terrifying non-terrifying — transcending fear itself.

apāpakāśinī.root: अ (not) + पाप (sin/evil) + काशिनी (revealing/shining) — from √काश् (to shine, to appear, to be visible)

grammar.base: अपापकाशिन् (apāpakāśin — not revealing evil, manifesting purity)
panini: P 3.2.142 — the ini-pratyaya from √काश् forms the agent adjective: kāśin (one who shines/reveals)
P 2.2.6 — nañ-compound: a + pāpakāśin → apāpakāśin
P 4.1.5 — feminine ṅīṣ-pratyaya: apāpakāśin + ī → apāpakāśinī

meanings.english: Not revealing sin; manifesting purity; not making evil visible; shining without malevolence
meanings.nirukta: Compound of a + pāpa + kāśinī. Sāyaṇa parses: 'pāpam akāśayantī' — 'not making sin visible/manifest'. Alternatively: 'apāpam kāśayantī' — 'causing non-sin (purity) to shine forth'. Both readings are grammatically valid, and the Nirukta tradition embraces such productive ambiguity.
meanings.vedantic: This epithet describes Rudra's form that does not project evil or cause harmful manifestation. In Advaita, it suggests the nirguṇa Brahman that is beyond the categories of pāpa and puṇya. The form that is 'apāpakāśinī' is the svarūpa — the intrinsic nature that is pure consciousness without the projection of māyā.

śam.root: √शम् (to be calm, to be peaceful, to be auspicious) or शम् as an avyaya meaning 'peace/welfare'

grammar.forms: शन्तमया (śantamayā, superl. instr. sg. 'by the most peaceful'), शन्त (śanta, adj. 'peaceful' in compounds like गिरिशन्त)
panini: P 5.3.55 — tamac-pratyaya for superlative: शम् + तम → शन्तम (most peaceful)
P 3.2.141 — śānta from √śam + kta (past participle)

meanings.english: Peace; welfare; happiness; auspiciousness; tranquility
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.19 — listed among sukha-vācaka (happiness-denoting) words.
meanings.nirukta: Yāska derives from √śam — 'śāmyati upaśāmyati iti śam' (that by which one becomes calm/tranquil). The superlative śantama means 'most peaceful of all'.
meanings.vedantic: Śam is one of the fundamental Vedic blessings — 'śaṃ no astu dvipade śaṃ catuṣpade' (may there be peace for two-footed and four-footed). In the context of Śrī Rudram, asking to be seen by Rudra's 'śantamā tanū' (most peaceful form) is asking for the vision of Brahman that liberates.

giriśanta.root: गिरि (giri — mountain) + √शम् (śam — to be peaceful/dwell) — one who dwells peacefully on the mountain

grammar.base: गिरिशन्त (giriśanta — mountain-dweller; O peaceful one of the mountain)
panini: P 2.1.32 — tatpuruṣa compound: giri + śanta (dwelling on the mountain)
P 3.2.37 — śanta as a verbal adjective from √śam in upapada compound

meanings.english: O dweller of the mountain; O mountain-lord; one who rests on the mountain
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 5.3 — compound deity-epithet. 'Giri' is listed among pṛthivī-vācaka (earth/terrain-denoting) words in Nighantu 1.1.
meanings.nirukta: Compound of giri (mountain) + śanta (from √śam, dwelling/at peace). Sāyaṇa explains: 'girau śāmyati nivasati iti giriśantaḥ' — one who dwells/reposes on the mountain. The mountain represents both the physical Kailāsa and the immovable stability of the Supreme.
meanings.amara_kosha: Related: Amarakośa 1.1.29 lists 'girīśa' among Śiva's names. गिरि in Amarakośa 2.3.2 (Bhūmi-varga): 'adriḥ giriḥ śailaḥ' — mountain synonyms.
meanings.vedantic: The mountain symbolizes the immovable, eternal nature of Brahman. Giriśanta is Rudra as the one established in the unshakable ground of being — 'acala' (immovable). The Kena Upaniṣad (3.12) places the divine revelation on a mountain — Umā Haimavatī appears on the Himālaya, connecting mountain-dwelling with supreme knowledge.

panini: P 3.2.183 — tra-pratyaya from √trā (to protect) in upapada compound: giri + tra → giritra

meanings.english: O protector of the mountain; O mountain-guardian
meanings.nirukta: Compound: giri + tra (from √trā 'to protect'). 'Girim trāyate iti giritraḥ' — one who protects the mountain. Alternatively, 'girau trāyate' — one who protects [beings] on the mountain. Sāyaṇa glosses as 'parvatānām pālaka' (guardian of mountains).
meanings.vedantic: Giritra is Rudra in his role as cosmic protector. The mountain represents the manifest world (jagat), and its protector is the sustaining aspect of Brahman (sthiti-kāraka). The vocative address shifts from 'giriśanta' (dweller) to 'giritra' (protector) — escalating the plea from recognition to active guardianship.

giriśa.root: गिरि (giri — mountain) + √शी (śī — to lie, to sleep, to rest)

grammar.base: गिरिश (giriśa — mountain-lord; one who reclines on the mountain)
panini: P 3.2.15 — śa-pratyaya from √śī in upapada compound: giri + śa → giriśa
P 6.4.20 — dropping of ī before the suffix

meanings.english: O mountain-lord; one who reclines/resides on the mountain; lord of Kailāsa
meanings.nirukta: Compound: giri + śa (from √śī 'to recline/sleep/rest'). 'Girau śete iti giriśaḥ' — one who reclines on the mountain. The epithet conveys sovereignty — the lord who rests upon the mountain as his throne.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.29 — गिरीशः listed explicitly among Śiva's names: 'giriśaḥ girīśaḥ'.
meanings.vedantic: Giriśa connotes lordship over the earth/material world (pṛthivī-tattva). The mountain as throne represents sovereignty over all material existence. In mantra 5, the devotees address Rudra as Giriśa while declaring they speak to him with auspicious words — approaching the lord with śivena vacasā.

abhi.root: अभि (abhi — preverb/prefix: towards, over, upon, in the direction of)

panini: P 1.4.58 — abhi classified as an upasarga (preverb) when prefixed to a verbal root
P 1.4.59 — upasargas modify the meaning of the verb they attach to

meanings.english: Towards; upon; over; facing; in the direction of
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.29 — listed among upasargas (preverbs) in the particle list.
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 1.3 — Yāska discusses how upasargas like abhi modify verbal meaning. Abhi adds the sense of 'directed towards' or 'facing'. In 'abhicākashīhi' it intensifies — 'shine upon us, face us with your benign form'.
meanings.vedantic: Abhi as 'towards' establishes the directionality of divine grace — the devotee asks Rudra to turn his benign aspect towards them.

cakāśīhi.root: √काश् (kāś — to shine, to be visible, to appear, to look) — intensive/frequentative (yaṅanta)

grammar.base: चकाश् (cakāś — intensive of √kāś: to shine brightly, to look intently)
grammar.form: 2nd singular imperative intensive (yaṅanta loṭ) — 'shine forth! look upon us!'
panini: P 3.1.22 — yaṅ-pratyaya for intensive (frequentative): काश् → चाकाश्/चकाश् (with reduplication per P 6.1.9)
P 3.4.87 — imperative hi: चकाश् + हि → चकशीहि

meanings.english: Shine upon us; look upon us with radiance; appear before us benevolently; gaze upon us
meanings.nirukta: The intensive (yaṅanta) of √kāś intensifies the basic meaning 'to shine' into 'to shine forth brilliantly / to gaze intently'. With abhi-prefix: 'abhicākashīhi' — 'shine upon us, turn your radiant gaze toward us'. Sāyaṇa glosses: 'asmān prati prakāśasva dṛśyasva vā' — 'shine toward us or become visible to us'.
meanings.vedantic: The intensive form conveys urgency — not merely 'look' but 'look intently, continuously'. This is the devotee's prayer for darśana — the divine vision. In Vedāntic terms, it is asking the Supreme to reveal its true (śiva, aghora) nature to the devotee. The act of the divine 'looking upon' the devotee is itself liberation — 'sa dṛṣṭo mṛḍayāti naḥ' (mantra 8).

hasta.root: √हस् (has — to seize, to grasp) + त (ta — suffix)

grammar.forms: हस्ते (haste, loc. sg. 'in the hand')
panini: √हस् + त (Uṇādi 3.86) → हस्त
P 7.3.102 — locative singular of a-stem: hasta + i → haste

meanings.english: Hand; grasp; the instrument of holding and wielding
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 2.4 — listed among aṅga-nāmāni (body-part names): hasta, kara, pāṇi.
meanings.nirukta: Derived from √has — 'hasati gṛhṇāti iti hastaḥ' (that which grasps/seizes). The hand is the fundamental instrument of action.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.6.73 (Aṅga-varga) — 'hastaḥ pāṇiḥ karaḥ' — hasta, pāṇi, and kara are synonyms for hand.
meanings.vedantic: Rudra's hand holds the arrow — 'haste bibharṣi' (you bear in hand). The hand represents kartṛtva (agency). In mantra 13, 'haste babhūva te dhanuḥ' — the weapon has come to rest in his hand. The prayer asks for that hand's action to be transformed from destruction to protection.

bhṛ.root: √भृ (bhṛ — to bear, to carry, to support, to maintain) — juhotyādi, ubhayapada

grammar.forms: बिभर्षि (bibharṣi, 2nd sg. present reduplicative 'you bear/carry')
panini: P 3.1.1 — juhotyādi-gaṇa (3rd class) verbs reduplicate: भृ → बिभृ
P 7.2.76 — reduplication rules: भृ → बिभर् (with i for reduplicant vowel)
P 6.1.190 — bibhar + si → bibharṣi (ṣ-change per P 8.3.59)

meanings.english: You bear; you carry; you hold; you support
meanings.nirukta: √bhṛ means 'to carry, to sustain'. 'Bibharṣi' (reduplicative present) emphasizes continuous bearing — 'you continually hold [the arrow]'. The reduplication indicates habitual or characterizing action — Rudra is defined by his weapon-bearing.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.1.26 — √bhṛ listed among dhāraṇa-vācaka (carrying/supporting) verbs.
meanings.vedantic: The bearing of the weapon is not merely physical but ontological — Rudra sustains the cosmic order through his martial readiness. The prayer asks him to transform the arrow he bears from threatening to protective.

astave.root: √अस् (as — to throw, to hurl, to shoot) — divādi, parasmaipada (distinct from √अस् 'to be')

grammar.form: dative infinitive — 'for hurling, for the purpose of shooting'
panini: P 3.4.9 — tumun-pratyaya or Vedic dative infinitive in -tave: अस् + तवे → अस्तवे
P 3.3.10 — Vedic infinitive formations in -tave (parallel to -tum in classical Sanskrit)

meanings.english: For hurling; for the purpose of throwing/shooting
meanings.nirukta: The Vedic dative infinitive from √as (to throw). 'Iṣum haste bibharṣi astave' — 'you bear the arrow in hand for the purpose of hurling'. The infinitive expresses intention — the arrow is held ready to be shot.
meanings.vedantic: The prayer acknowledges Rudra's readiness to strike — the arrow is held 'for hurling'. By acknowledging the intent, the devotee then asks for that intent to be made śiva (auspicious) — transforming the purpose from destruction to protection.

kṛ.root: √कृ (kṛ — to do, to make, to perform) — tanādi, ubhayapada

grammar.forms: कुरु (kuru, 2nd sg. imperative 'make! do!'), अकरम् (akaram, 1st sg. imperfect 'I did/made')
panini: P 3.4.87 — loṭ (imperative): कृ + हि → कुरु (with special stem per P 6.1.59)
P 3.2.111 — laṅ (imperfect): अ + कृ + अम् → अकरम्

meanings.english: Make; do; render; perform
meanings.nirukta: The most fundamental action-verb. In mantra 4: 'śivāṃ giritra tāṃ kuru' — 'O mountain-protector, make that [arrow] auspicious'. The imperative 'kuru' is a direct petition.
meanings.vedantic: The prayer 'tāṃ kuru śivām' asks Rudra to transform his arrow's nature — this is the Vedic understanding that the divine can alter the fundamental character of reality through will. In Vedāntic terms, it is asking the cosmic agent to exercise his freedom (svātantrya) benevolently.

grammar.form: prohibitive particle used with injunctive or aorist
panini: P 3.3.175 — मा used with liṅ (optative) or luṅ (aorist) for prohibition
P 8.1.29 — mā is a pragṛhya (accented) particle, unlike most nipātas

meanings.english: Do not; may [you] not; refrain from
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.29 — listed among niṣedhārthaka avyayas (prohibitive particles).
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 1.4 — mā is the Vedic particle of negation specific to commands/wishes. Unlike 'na' (factual negation), 'mā' is volitional — 'may you not, I implore you not to'. In 'mā hiṃsīḥ' — 'do not harm'.
meanings.vedantic: The prohibitive 'mā' in prayer is not a command to the divine but a humble entreaty — 'please, do not'. It presupposes Rudra's power to harm and asks for voluntary restraint. In Vedānta, this is the recognition that grace is freely given, not demanded.

hiṃs.root: √हिंस् (hiṃs — to hurt, to harm, to injure, to kill) — derived from √हन् (han — to strike) with sa-pratyaya

grammar.forms: हिंसीः (hiṃsīḥ, 2nd sg. aorist injunctive 'may you harm/do not harm')
panini: P 3.1.6 — desiderative-like sa-formation from √हन्: han → hiṃsa (P 7.4.54 for nasal insertion)
P 3.3.175 — with मा, the aorist/injunctive expresses prohibition: मा हिंसीः = 'do not harm'

meanings.english: To hurt; to harm; to injure; to kill
meanings.nirukta: Derived from √han (to strike/kill) with intensifying sa-suffix. The Nirukta tradition reads hiṃs as more comprehensive than han — it includes all forms of injury, not just killing.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.1.18 — हिंसा listed meaning 'pīḍā' (pain, torment): 'hiṃsā pīḍā'.
meanings.vedantic: The plea 'mā hiṃsīḥ puruṣaṃ jagat' — 'do not harm man or the living world' — is one of the foundational prayers for ahiṃsā (non-violence) in Vedic literature. It recognizes that Rudra has the power to destroy but asks him to restrain that power. In Advaita, the 'puruṣa' and 'jagat' are both manifestations of Brahman — to harm them would be for Brahman to harm itself.

puruṣa.root: पुरि शेते इति पुरुषः (one who dwells in the city [of the body]); or from √pṝ (to fill)

grammar.base: पुरुष (puruṣa — man, person, being, the cosmic Person)
grammar.forms: पुरुषम् (puruṣam, acc. sg.)
panini: P 6.3.137 — puruṣa as a prātipadika (nominal stem)
P 1.4.103 — puruṣa in grammatical sense: prathama/madhyama/uttama puruṣa (third/second/first person)

meanings.english: Man; person; being; the cosmic Person; spirit; soul; individual self
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 2.3 — listed among manuṣya-vācaka (man-denoting) words.
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 2.3 — Yāska gives: 'puri śayānāt puruṣaḥ' or 'puri sadanāt' — one who dwells/lies in the pura (city, i.e., the body). Also from √pṝ — 'pūrayati iti puruṣaḥ' (one who fills/pervades). The Puruṣa Sūkta (RV 10.90) establishes Puruṣa as the cosmic being from whom all creation emanates.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.6.1 — पुरुषः listed as primary word for 'man': 'puruṣaḥ pumān manuṣyaḥ mānavaḥ manujah naraḥ' — man-synonyms.
meanings.vedantic: In Sāṅkhya-Vedānta, Puruṣa is the conscious principle distinct from Prakṛti. In Advaita, the jīva-puruṣa is ultimately identical with Brahman. The prayer 'mā hiṃsīḥ puruṣam' protects both the individual and the cosmic Person — Rudra is asked not to harm his own manifestation.

jagat.root: √गम् (gam — to go, to move) with reduplication — 'that which moves/lives'

grammar.base: जगत् (jagat — the moving world, living beings, the universe)
panini: P 3.2.178 — reduplicative formation from √gam: ga + gam + at → jagat (with P 6.1.9 reduplication rules)
P 7.1.70 — t-stem declension: nom./acc. sg. jagat

meanings.english: The world; the moving universe; living beings; all that moves and lives
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 1.1 — listed among loka-vācaka (world-denoting) and gati-vācaka (motion-denoting) words.
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 2.1 — Yāska: 'gacchati iti jagat' (that which moves/goes — hence the world of living beings). The reduplication (ja-gat from √gam) indicates continuous motion — the world is perpetually in flux. Contrasted with 'sthāvara' (the stationary).
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.7 — जगत् listed meaning 'viśva' (universe): 'lokaḥ jagat viśvam'.
meanings.vedantic: In Vedānta, jagat is the empirical world of name and form (nāma-rūpa) — real from the vyāvahārika (practical) standpoint, ultimately mithyā (apparent) from the pāramārthika (absolute) standpoint. The prayer protects both puruṣa (conscious beings) and jagat (the entire phenomenal world) — nothing should be excluded from Rudra's grace.

vacas.root: √वच् (vac — to speak, to say, to tell)

grammar.forms: वचसा (vacasā, instr. sg. 'with/by speech')
panini: √वच् + असुन् (Uṇādi 4.188) → वचस्
P 7.1.75 — s-stem (asanta) declension: instr. sg. वचसा

meanings.english: Speech; word; voice; utterance; what is spoken
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 1.11 — listed among vāk-vācaka (speech-denoting) words: vacas, vāk, gir, uktha.
meanings.nirukta: Derived from √vac — 'ucyate iti vacaḥ' (that which is spoken). In mantra 5: 'śivena vacasā' — 'with auspicious speech'. The instrumental case indicates the medium through which the devotees approach Rudra.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.6.1 — वचः listed in the Vāk-varga (speech section): 'vacaḥ vāṇī vāk gīḥ' — speech synonyms.
meanings.vedantic: In Vedic theology, speech (vāc) is a divine power — Vāk Devī. 'Śivena vacasā' means approaching the divine with words that are themselves transformed into auspiciousness. The power of mantra lies in the word itself being a carrier of the sacred.

grammar.forms: त्वम् (tvam, nom. sg.), त्वा (tvā, enclitic acc. sg. 'you' as object)
panini: P 8.1.22 — त्वा is the enclitic accusative form
P 7.2.97 — nominative singular tvam

meanings.english: You (nominative); you (accusative, as tvā)
meanings.nirukta: The full nominative form 'tvam' is emphatic — 'you yourself'. In mantra 5: 'tvā giriśācchā vadāmasi' — 'we speak to you, O Giriśa, towards [you]'.
meanings.vedantic: The pronoun 'tvam' in 'tat tvam asi' (Chāndogya 6.8.7) is the jīvātman. Here it addresses Rudra directly, establishing the intimate I-Thou relationship of devotion.

accha.root: अच्छ (accha — towards, in the direction of) — Vedic adverb/postposition

panini: P 1.4.74 — accha classified among karmapravacanīya words that govern accusative
P 1.4.84 — accha as a gati (directional element) with verbs of motion/speech

meanings.english: Towards; in the direction of; facing
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.29 — listed among gati-vācaka avyayas (directional particles).
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 1.9 — accha is a Vedic directional particle meaning 'towards, facing'. Used with verbs of speaking: 'acchā vadāmasi' = 'we speak towards [you]'. Rare in classical Sanskrit, common in Vedic.
meanings.vedantic: The directional particle emphasizes that the prayer is aimed at Rudra — not a general invocation but a focused, directed address.

vad.root: √वद् (vad — to speak, to say, to utter, to address) — bhvādi, parasmaipada

grammar.forms: वदामसि (vadāmasi, 1st pl. present 'we speak/address')
panini: P 3.4.78 — laṭ (present tense), uttama puruṣa (1st person), bahu-vacana (plural): वद् + मस् → वदामसि

meanings.english: We speak; we address; we utter; we declare
meanings.nirukta: √vad — to speak. 'Vadāmasi' = 'we address/declare'. The first person plural includes the entire worshipping community.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.1.33 — √वद् listed meaning 'bhāṣate' (to speak).
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 5: 'śivena vacasā tvā giriśācchā vadāmasi' — 'with auspicious speech we address you, O Giriśa'. The act of Vedic recitation is itself a form of worship — the words, properly chanted, become the offering.

yathā.root: यद् (yad — relative pronoun) + था (thā — manner suffix)

grammar.base: यथा (yathā — in order that, so that, as, like)
panini: P 5.3.23 — thāl-pratyaya from yad: yad + thā → yathā (in which manner, so that)

meanings.english: In order that; so that; as; in such a way that
meanings.nirukta: Yāska treats yathā as expressing purpose or manner. In mantra 5: 'yathā naḥ sarvam ij jagat ayakṣmaṃ sumanā asat' — 'so that all this world of ours may be free from disease and of good mind'.
meanings.vedantic: Yathā introduces the desired outcome of the prayer — establishing the causal link between approaching Rudra with auspicious speech and the world becoming free of disease.

grammar.forms: सर्वम् (sarvam, neut. nom/acc. sg. 'all/everything'), सर्वान् (sarvān, masc. acc. pl. 'all [of them]')
panini: P 1.1.27 — sarva is classified as a sarvanāman (pronoun), hence takes pronominal declension
P 7.1.14 — sarva declines like a pronoun, not a regular adjective

meanings.english: All; every; entire; whole; complete
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.1 — listed among words denoting totality/completeness.
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta treats sarva as expressing comprehensive inclusion — nothing is left out.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.1.1 — सर्वः listed as a sarvanāman: 'sarvaḥ viśvaḥ ubhau' — sarva, viśva, and ubha are words for 'all'.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 5: 'sarvam ij jagat' — 'the entire world indeed'. The use of sarva with the emphatic particle it/id underscores that the prayer encompasses all of creation without exception.

id.root: इद् (id — emphatic particle: indeed, verily, just)

panini: P 1.1.37 — classified as nipāta
P 8.3.15 — final d → t/ḥ in pause (id → it before consonants)

meanings.english: Indeed; verily; just; exactly; precisely (emphatic particle)
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.29 — listed among avadharaṇārthaka avyayas (emphatic particles).
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 1.4 — Yāska classifies id as an avadhāraṇa-nipāta (restrictive/emphatic particle). In 'sarvam it jagat' it emphasizes: 'the entire world, indeed — without exception'.
meanings.vedantic: The particle id/it adds emphasis to sarva — 'all this world, truly all of it' should be free from disease. No part of creation is excluded from the prayer's scope.

ayakṣma.root: अ (not) + यक्ष्म (yakṣma — disease, consumption, wasting illness)

grammar.forms: अयक्ष्मम् (ayakṣmam, neut. acc. sg.), अयक्ष्मया (ayakṣmayā, fem. instr. sg.)
panini: P 6.2.155 — nañ-tatpuruṣa: a + yakṣma → ayakṣma
P 2.2.6 — negative compound formation

meanings.english: Free from disease; healthy; without consumption/wasting; without affliction
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 2.9 — yakṣma listed among roga-vācaka (disease-denoting) words. Ayakṣma is its negation.
meanings.nirukta: Yakṣma is a general term for wasting disease (consumption/tuberculosis in later medical texts). Ayakṣma = absence of such disease. In Vedic context, yakṣma encompasses all debilitating illness — 'yat kṣīṇaṃ bhavati tat yakṣma' (that which causes wasting is yakṣma).
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 5: 'ayakṣmaṃ sumanā asat' — 'may [the world] be free from disease and of good mind'. Health of body (ayakṣma) and mind (sumanās) together constitute well-being. In Vedānta, the ultimate yakṣma is avidyā (ignorance) — freedom from spiritual disease is mokṣa.

grammar.base: सुमनस् (sumanas — of good mind, well-disposed, cheerful)
grammar.forms: सुमनाः (sumanāḥ, nom. sg. masc./neut.)
panini: P 2.2.24 — bahuvrīhi: su + manas → sumanas (one whose mind is good)
P 7.1.75 — s-stem declension: nom. sg. sumanāḥ

meanings.english: Of good mind; well-disposed; cheerful; benevolent; happy-minded
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.19 — su- prefix words listed among kalyāṇa-vācaka terms.
meanings.nirukta: Compound: su (good/auspicious) + manas (mind). One whose mind (manas) is good (su) — cheerful, benevolent, well-intentioned.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.1.5 — सुमनस् as adjective meaning 'prasanna-citta' (clear/cheerful-minded). Also Amarakośa 2.4.75 — सुमनस् as noun meaning 'puṣpa' (flower) — flowers are 'of good mind/disposition' because they give pleasure.
meanings.vedantic: In mantras 5 and 11: the world and Rudra himself are asked to be sumanās — of good mind/disposition. This pairs with ayakṣma — health of body and mind together. In Vedānta, sumanas is the state of a mind free from kleśas (afflictions) — the prerequisite for self-knowledge.

adhi_vac.root: अधि + √वच् (adhi + vac — to speak on behalf of, to advocate, to intercede)

grammar.forms: अध्यवोचत् (adhyavocat, 3rd sg. imperfect/aorist 'he spoke forth'), अधिवक्ता (adhivaktā, nom. sg. agent noun 'the advocate/intercessor')
panini: P 3.2.1 — agent noun formation: अधि + √वच् + तृच् → अधिवक्तृ, nom. अधिवक्ता
P 3.2.111 — luṅ (aorist): अ + अधि + वच् + त् → अध्यवोचत् (with vriddhi per P 7.2.3)

meanings.english: He spoke forth; he declared/advocated; the intercessor; the one who speaks on behalf of
meanings.nirukta: Compound verb: adhi (over/on behalf of) + √vac (to speak). Adhivaktā = one who speaks on behalf of others, an advocate/patron. Sāyaṇa: 'adhikṛtya vaktā' — one who speaks with authority. In mantra 6, Rudra is 'prathamaḥ daivyaḥ bhiṣak adhivaktā' — the first divine physician and advocate.
meanings.vedantic: Rudra as adhivaktā is the divine advocate who intercedes on behalf of the suffering. As the first (prathama) divine physician (daivyo bhiṣak), he is the primal healer — establishing that healing is a divine function, not merely human.

prathama.root: प्र (pra — forth, first) + थम (superlative suffix) — from √prath (to extend, to spread)

grammar.forms: प्रथमः (prathamaḥ, nom. sg. masc.)
panini: P 5.2.51 — thamac-pratyaya for ordinal: pra + thama → prathama
P 2.2.11 — ordinals in compounds

meanings.english: First; foremost; primordial; chief; the original
meanings.nirukta: From √prath (to extend/spread) — 'prathate iti prathamaḥ' (that which extends first, the foremost). Establishes temporal and hierarchical priority.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.3.167 — प्रथमः listed meaning 'ādi' (beginning, first): 'prathamaḥ ādyaḥ pūrvaḥ'.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 6: Rudra is 'prathamaḥ daivyo bhiṣak' — the first/primordial divine physician. This establishes Rudra-Śiva as the origin of healing — all medicine traces back to the divine healer. In the Śaiva tradition, Śiva is the Ādi-vaidya (primordial doctor) who cures the disease of saṃsāra.

daivya.root: देव (deva — god, shining one) + य (secondary derivation suffix)

grammar.base: दैव्य (daivya — divine, belonging to the gods, celestial)
grammar.forms: दैव्यः (daivyaḥ, nom. sg. masc.)
panini: P 4.2.48 — yat-pratyaya with vṛddhi: देव → दैव → दैव्य (relating to/belonging to the devas)

meanings.english: Divine; celestial; belonging to the gods; of divine origin
meanings.nighantu: Related: Nighantu 5.1-5 (Daivata-kāṇḍa) — extensive listing of deva-names, establishing the category 'daivya'.
meanings.nirukta: From deva — 'devānām ayam iti daivyaḥ' (belonging to the devas). The vṛddhi suffix (-ya with āi) indicates derivation/belonging.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.3.127 — दैव्यः listed meaning 'devata-sambandhi' (relating to deity).
meanings.vedantic: Qualifies bhiṣak (physician) — the healing is not worldly but divine in nature. This is not human medicine but cosmic restoration.

bhiṣaj.root: √भिष् (bhiṣ — to heal, to cure) + अच् (ac — suffix)

grammar.forms: भिषक् (bhiṣak, nom. sg. with k-ending per P 8.2.36)
panini: P 8.2.36 — final j → k in pause: bhiṣaj → bhiṣak

meanings.english: Physician; healer; doctor; one who cures
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.13 — listed among cikitsaka-vācaka (healer-denoting) words.
meanings.nirukta: From √bhiṣ — 'bhiṣajyati cikitsati iti bhiṣak' (one who heals/treats). Yāska connects it to the concept of restoring wholeness.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.9.1 — भिषक् listed as synonym of vaidya: 'bhiṣak vaidyaḥ cikitsakaḥ' — physician synonyms.
meanings.vedantic: Rudra as the primordial physician heals not just bodily disease but spiritual affliction. The Śvetāśvatara Upaniṣad presents Rudra-Śiva as the ultimate healer of the disease of transmigration (bhava-roga). The Ayurvedic tradition traces its origin to Lord Śiva through Dhanvantari.

ahi.root: अहि (ahi — serpent, snake; also a class of demons/diseases)

grammar.forms: अहीन् (ahīn, acc. pl. masc. 'the serpents/serpent-demons')
panini: P 7.1.73 — i-stem declension: acc. pl. अहीन् (i → ī before n-ending)

meanings.english: Serpent; snake; a class of serpent-demons; also metaphorically: diseases, afflictions
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 1.9 — listed among sarpa-vācaka (serpent-denoting) words: ahi, sarpa, nāga.
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 4.23 — 'ahīn sambhajayati' — related to supernatural serpent-beings. In Vedic, 'ahi' can mean both literal serpents and metaphorical forces of obstruction (cf. Vṛtra as the arch-ahi in Ṛgveda).
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.8.6 — अहिः listed among sarpa-names: 'sarpaḥ pṛdākuḥ bhujagaḥ bhujaṅgaḥ ahiḥ' — serpent synonyms.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 6, Rudra destroys 'ahīn' (serpent-demons) — representing forces of evil and disease. In Vedāntic symbolism, the serpent represents saṃsāra's coiling bondage — Rudra as divine physician destroys these binding forces.

ca.root: च (ca — and, also, both) — indeclinable conjunction

grammar.form: indeclinable conjunction (always follows the word it connects)
panini: P 1.1.37 — classified as nipāta
P 2.2.29 — ca as coordinator in dvandva compounds and serial constructions
P 8.1.63 — ca is always enclitic (anudātta)

meanings.english: And; also; both; as well as
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.29 — listed among samuccayārthaka nipātas (conjunctive particles).
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 1.4 — ca is the primary samuccaya (additive) particle. Unlike 'uta' which has emphatic force, 'ca' is neutral conjunction.
meanings.vedantic: In Vedic syntax, ca typically follows the word it connects (postpositive). In mantra 6: 'ahīṃśca sarvān jambhayan sarvāṃśca yātudhānyaḥ' — 'destroying both all serpent-demons and all sorcerers'.

jambh.root: √जम्भ् (jambh / jambh — to crush, to destroy, to snap/bite) — bhvādi

grammar.forms: जम्भयन् (jambhayan, present participle causative nom. sg. 'crushing/destroying')
panini: P 3.1.26 — causative (ṇic): जम्भ् + णिच् → जम्भय
P 3.2.124 — present participle (śatṛ): जम्भय + शतृ → जम्भयन्

meanings.english: Crushing; destroying; snapping with jaws; devouring
meanings.nirukta: √jambh — 'jambhayati nāśayati' (to crush, to destroy with teeth/jaws). The causative form intensifies — 'causing destruction'. Related to jambha (jaw/tooth). Sāyaṇa glosses: 'nāśayan' (destroying).
meanings.vedantic: Rudra as the divine physician 'crushes' diseases and sorceries. The verb jambhayan (present participle) indicates ongoing action — Rudra continuously destroys evil forces, not just once but perpetually.

yātudhānī.root: यातु (yātu — sorcery, black magic) + √धा (dhā — to hold, to possess) + ई (feminine suffix)

grammar.base: यातुधान (yātudhāna — sorcerer, one who possesses sorcery); fem. यातुधानी
grammar.forms: यातुधान्यः (yātudhānyaḥ, fem. acc. pl. 'the sorceresses')
panini: P 3.2.103 — upapada compound: yātu + √dhā + āna → yātudhāna
P 4.1.5 — feminine ṅīṣ: yātudhānī
P 7.1.20 — acc. pl. fem.: yātudhānyaḥ

meanings.english: Sorceresses; female sorcerers; those who wield black magic; demonesses
meanings.nighantu: Related to the rākṣasa/piśāca category in Vedic demonology. Nighantu 2.17 — yātu listed among abhicāra-vācaka (sorcery-denoting) words.
meanings.nirukta: Compound: yātu (sorcery/witchcraft) + dhāna (possessing/wielding). 'Yātum dhārayati iti yātudhānaḥ' — one who wields sorcery. The feminine plural in mantra 6 refers to an entire class of malevolent beings.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.62 — related: rākṣasa terms in the asura-varga.
meanings.vedantic: Yātudhānī represents the forces of māyā in its malevolent form — the sorceries that delude and harm. Rudra as divine physician destroys not just physical disease (ahi) but also supernatural malevolence (yātudhānī). In Vedānta, these represent the vāsanās (subtle impressions) that perpetuate bondage.

adas.root: अदस् (adas — that yonder, the distant one) — remote demonstrative pronoun

grammar.forms: असौ (asau, masc. nom. sg. 'that one yonder')
panini: P 1.1.27 — adas classified as sarvanāman
P 7.2.107 — special suppletion: adas → asau (nom. sg. masc.)
P 7.2.102 — remote demonstrative declension paradigm

meanings.english: That one yonder; the one over there (pointing to something distant/visible but removed)
meanings.nirukta: Adas is the remote demonstrative — pointing to what is visible but distant. In mantras 7-8: 'asau yaḥ' — 'that one yonder who [is coppery, ruddy...]' — pointing to the visible sun, which is identified with Rudra.
meanings.vedantic: The remote demonstrative 'asau' in mantras 7-8 points to the sun — identifying the visible solar deity with Rudra. This is the Vedic technique of adhyātma-upāsana: using the visible (sun) as a gateway to contemplate the invisible (Brahman/Rudra). The Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad uses similar pointing language for Brahman.

grammar.forms: ताम्रः (tāmraḥ, nom. sg. masc.)
panini: P 4.2.2 — tāmra as a color adjective derived from the material tāmra (copper)

meanings.english: Copper-colored; coppery; reddish; the color of copper
meanings.nighantu: Related: Nighantu 1.5 — color terms. Tāmra among lohita-vācaka (red-denoting) words.
meanings.nirukta: Named after the metal tāmra (copper) for its characteristic reddish hue. Describes Rudra in his solar form — the rising sun has a copper/red tinge.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.9.97 — ताम्रम् listed as a metal: 'tāmraṃ śulbam' (copper). As color: applied to objects of copper hue.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 7, Rudra is described as tāmra (coppery), aruṇa (ruddy), and babhru (tawny) — the colors of the sun at different phases. This solar identification connects Rudra to the Āditya-principle (consciousness that illuminates). The Chāndogya Upaniṣad (1.6.6) describes the puruṣa in the sun as 'golden-bearded, golden all over' — Rudra's coppery hue is this same solar radiance.

aruṇa.root: √ṛ / √ar (to go, to move, to rise) + uṇa (suffix) — the ruddy dawn light

grammar.forms: अरुणः (aruṇaḥ, nom. sg. masc.)
panini: Uṇādi 3.53 — √ṛ + uṇa → aruṇa

meanings.english: Ruddy; reddish-brown; dawn-colored; the color of the rising sun
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 1.5 — listed among lohita-vācaka (red-denoting) words and also among sūrya-nāmāni (sun-names) in Nighantu 1.4.
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 12.16 — from √ṛ/ar — 'iyarti gacchati iti aruṇaḥ' (that which moves/rises). Refers to the ruddy glow of dawn/sunrise. Also the name of the charioteer of the sun (Sūrya).
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.3.28 — अरुणः listed as a name for the dawn and the sun's charioteer: 'aruṇo'nūruḥ kāśyapaḥ'.
meanings.vedantic: The dawn-color describes Rudra in his benevolent solar aspect — the sun that brings light after darkness. In Vedāntic meditation, the aruṇa-varṇa (dawn-colored) puruṣa in the sun is a form for upāsana of Brahman.

babhru.root: √भृ (bhṛ — to bear) with reduplication, or from babhru as a color-word (tawny, brown-golden)

grammar.forms: बभ्रुः (babhruḥ, nom. sg. masc.)
panini: Uṇādi 1.22 — reduplicative formation → babhru (u-stem)

meanings.english: Tawny; brown; golden-brown; the color of a brown cow or dark honey
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 1.5 — listed among color-denoting words, specifically in the lohita/piṅgala (reddish-brown) category.
meanings.nirukta: A Vedic color term for the warm brown-gold spectrum. Often applied to Soma, cattle, and solar deities. Related to √bhṛ — 'that which bears/carries [warmth/color]'.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.5.13 — बभ्रुः listed meaning 'kapilavarṇa' (tawny/brown color).
meanings.vedantic: The three colors — tāmra, aruṇa, babhru — describe the solar Rudra through his full color range from copper-red through ruddy to golden-brown. This threefold description mirrors the three states (avasthā-traya) or three guṇas manifest in the visible sun.

sumaṅgala.root: सु (su — good, excellent) + मङ्गल (maṅgala — auspiciousness, blessing)

grammar.forms: सुमङ्गलः (sumaṅgalaḥ, nom. sg. masc.)
panini: P 2.1.60 — karmadhāraya: su + maṅgala → sumaṅgala

meanings.english: Very auspicious; most blessed; supremely propitious; embodiment of good fortune
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.19 — maṅgala listed among kalyāṇa-vācaka words. The su- prefix intensifies.
meanings.nirukta: Compound: su (exceedingly) + maṅgala (auspiciousness). 'Suṣṭhu maṅgalam yasya saḥ' — one in whom auspiciousness abounds.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.4.25 — मङ्गलम् listed meaning 'puṇya' (merit/auspiciousness). Sumaṅgala is its intensified form.
meanings.vedantic: After describing Rudra's fearsome colors (tāmra, aruṇa, babhru), the verse pivots to 'sumaṅgalaḥ' — supremely auspicious. This juxtaposition is the theological core: the terrible IS the auspicious. The colors of the setting/rising sun, which might seem fierce, are declared to be sumaṅgala.

idam.root: इदम् (idam — this, this here) — proximate demonstrative pronoun

grammar.forms: इमाम् (imām, fem. acc. sg. 'this'), एनम् (enam, masc. acc. sg. 'him/this one' — special anaphoric form)
panini: P 1.1.27 — idam classified as sarvanāman
P 7.2.108-109 — special suppletion forms: idam → imam/imām
P 7.2.85 — enad-ādeśa: idam → enam/enām (anaphoric replacement)

meanings.english: This; this here; this very (proximate demonstrative pointing to something nearby)
meanings.nirukta: Idam points to what is immediate and present, contrasted with adas (remote). In mantra 7: 'imāṃ rudrāḥ' — 'these Rudras here [in all directions]'.
meanings.vedantic: The proximate 'idam' (this) vs remote 'adas' (that) establishes a spatial theology — Rudra is both 'asau' (that one yonder — the transcendent sun) and manifest 'here' (imāṃ — in all directions through the Rudras). This is the saguṇa-nirguṇa polarity.

abhitas.root: अभि (abhi — towards) + तस् (tas — directional suffix: from/on all sides)

grammar.base: अभितः (abhitaḥ — on all sides, all around, from every direction)
panini: P 5.3.7 — tasil-pratyaya from abhi: abhi + tas → abhitas (from/on all sides)

meanings.english: On all sides; all around; from every direction; everywhere around
meanings.nirukta: From abhi + tas-suffix meaning 'from the direction of'. Abhitaḥ = 'from all directions'. In mantra 7: Rudras are 'abhito dikṣu śritāḥ' — 'stationed in the directions all around'.
meanings.vedantic: The Rudras pervade all directions (dikṣu) — this omnipresence reflects Brahman's sarvagatatva (all-pervasiveness). Nothing and nowhere is outside Rudra's presence.

diś.root: √दिश् (diś — to point, to show, to direct)

grammar.forms: दिक्षु (dikṣu, loc. pl. 'in the directions')
panini: P 8.2.36 — final ś → k in certain environments (before ṣu): diś → dik before ṣu
P 7.1.20 — loc. pl. of consonant-stem: diś + su → dikṣu

meanings.english: Direction; quarter; cardinal point; the compass directions
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 1.7 — listed among deśa-vācaka (space/place-denoting) words.
meanings.nirukta: From √diś — 'diśati darśayati iti diś' (that which points/shows). The directions are 'pointed out' by the sun's movement.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.2.1 — दिक् listed with directional terms: 'diś kakubh āśā' — direction synonyms.
meanings.vedantic: The Rudras stationed in all directions represent the omnipresence of the divine. The Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad (3.9.4) associates directions with specific deities — here all directions belong to Rudra.

śri.root: √श्रि (śri — to resort to, to depend on, to be stationed, to lean upon) — bhvādi, ubhayapada

grammar.forms: श्रिताः (śritāḥ, past participle nom. pl. masc. 'stationed, having resorted to')
panini: P 3.2.102 — past participle (kta): श्रि + त → श्रित
P 7.1.17 — nom. pl. masc.: śritāḥ

meanings.english: Stationed; resorted to; dependent upon; leaning on; established in
meanings.nirukta: √śri — 'śrayati āśrayati' (to resort to, to be established in). 'Dikṣu śritāḥ' — stationed in the directions. The past participle indicates established presence — the Rudras are permanently stationed, not merely passing through.
meanings.vedantic: The Rudras 'stationed in the directions' (dikṣu śritāḥ) represent the cosmic guardians — Rudra's presence is not confined to one location but pervades the entire spatial framework.

sahasraśas.root: सहस्र (sahasra — thousand) + शस् (śas — distributive suffix: 'by the thousands')

grammar.base: सहस्रशस् (sahasraśas — by the thousands, in thousands)
grammar.forms: सहस्रशः (sahasraśaḥ, nom. form)
panini: P 5.4.42 — śas-pratyaya for distributive/multiplicative: sahasra + śas → sahasraśas (by thousands)

meanings.english: By the thousands; in thousands; thousandfold
meanings.nirukta: From sahasra (thousand) + śas (distributive). Indicates the innumerable multitude of Rudras — they are not one but thousands, pervading all space.
meanings.vedantic: The 'thousands' of Rudras reflect the infinite manifestations of the one Brahman. Though Rudra is one ('eko hi rudro na dvitīyāya tasthur' — Śvet. Up. 3.2), his manifestations are countless. This is the Vedāntic teaching of the one appearing as many.

ava_heda.root: अव (ava — down, away) + हेडस् (heḍas — wrath, anger, hostility) from √हीड् (hīḍ — to be angry)

grammar.base_verb: √ईम् (īm — to pray, to request) — ava + heḍaḥ + √īm
grammar.forms: अवैषां हेडः ईमहे (avaiṣāṃ hedaḥ īmahe — 'we pray away their wrath')
panini: P 3.4.78 — laṭ ātmanepada 1st pl.: √ईम् + महिन् → ईमहे
हेडस्: √हीड् + असुन् (Uṇādi 4.188) → हेडस् (wrath)

meanings.english: We propitiate away their anger; we pray to avert their wrath; we seek to pacify their hostility
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.12 — heḍas listed among krodha-vācaka (anger-denoting) words.
meanings.nirukta: Heḍas from √hīḍ — 'hīḍyate kopayate' (to be angry with). 'Ava... īmahe' = 'we pray downward/away'. The compound construction means 'we pray to propitiate their anger away from us'.
meanings.vedantic: The prayer to avert the Rudras' heḍas (wrath) is a plea for the transformation of divine anger into grace. In Vedānta, this 'wrath' is the karmic consequence of ignorance — propitiating it is seeking liberation from the fruits of past actions.

avasarpati.root: अव (ava — down) + √सृप् (sṛp — to creep, to glide, to move)

grammar.base: अव√सृप् (avasṛp — to descend, to glide down, to move downward)
grammar.forms: अवसर्पति (avasarpati, 3rd sg. present 'he descends/glides down')
panini: P 3.4.78 — laṭ: अव + सृप् + ति → अवसर्पति (with guṇa per P 7.3.84)

meanings.english: He descends; he glides down; he moves downward (said of the sun setting or moving through the sky)
meanings.nirukta: Compound verb: ava (downward) + √sṛp (to creep/glide). Describes the sun's movement across the sky — gliding/traveling downward. Sāyaṇa: 'avagatim prāpnoti' — 'attains downward movement'.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 8: 'asau yo'vasarpati' — 'that one yonder who descends/traverses' — describes Rudra as the sun moving through the sky. The descending sun is identified with Rudra — even in 'going down' the divine maintains sovereignty. In Vedānta, this descent parallels the avaroha (descent) of Brahman into manifestation.

nīlagrīva.root: नील (nīla — blue, dark blue) + ग्रीवा (grīvā — neck, throat)

grammar.forms: नीलग्रीवः (nīlagrīvaḥ, nom. sg.), नीलग्रीवाय (nīlagrīvāya, dat. sg.)
panini: P 2.2.24 — bahuvrīhi: nīla + grīvā → nīlagrīva (one whose neck is blue)
P 2.2.35 — the compound takes masculine gender matching the person described, not the final member

meanings.english: Blue-necked; blue-throated (epithet of Śiva/Rudra referring to his swallowing of the hālāhala poison)
meanings.nighantu: Component: Nighantu 2.4 — grīvā listed among aṅga-nāmāni (body-part names). Nīla among varṇa-vācaka (color-denoting) words.
meanings.nirukta: Bahuvrīhi: 'nīlā grīvā yasya saḥ' — one whose throat is blue/dark. The blue neck is explained by the Purāṇic narrative of Śiva swallowing the hālāhala (cosmic poison) during samudra-manthana (churning of the ocean) — his throat turned blue from holding the poison.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.29 — नीलकण्ठः (nīlakaṇṭha — blue-throated) listed as one of Śiva's names. Grīvā/Kaṇṭha are synonyms: Amarakośa 2.6.92 — 'grīvā kaṇṭhaḥ' — neck synonyms.
meanings.vedantic: The blue neck symbolizes Rudra's capacity to absorb the world's poison (suffering/evil) without being destroyed by it. In Vedānta, this represents the jñānī (knower of Brahman) who remains untouched by the world's afflictions even while present within it — like a lotus in water.

grammar.base: विलोहित (vilohita — intensely red, very red, blood-red)
grammar.forms: विलोहितः (vilohitaḥ, nom. sg. masc.)
panini: P 2.2.18 — vi as an intensifying prefix with adjectives

meanings.english: Intensely red; very red; blood-red; crimson
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 1.5 — lohita listed among rakta-vācaka (red-denoting) words.
meanings.nirukta: Vi- intensifies lohita (red): 'viśeṣeṇa lohitaḥ' — specially/intensely red. Describes the deep crimson of the setting sun or of Rudra's form. Lohita from √ruh/loh — related to 'rudhira' (blood).
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.5.13 — लोहितः listed as a color: 'lohitaḥ raktaḥ' — red synonyms.
meanings.vedantic: Vi-lohita (intensely red) describes Rudra-as-sun in full crimson glory. The vivid redness contrasts with the nīla (blue) of the neck — the deity encompasses the full spectrum. In Vedāntic symbolism, red represents rajas (activity/passion), blue represents sattva (purity) — Rudra transcends both while manifesting through both.

gopa.root: गो (go — cow, earth) + √पा (pā — to protect, to guard)

grammar.forms: गोपाः (gopāḥ, nom. pl. masc. 'the cowherds')
panini: P 3.2.4 — upapada compound: go + √pā + ka → gopa (protector of cows)

meanings.english: Cowherd; cattle-keeper; guardian; one who tends and protects
meanings.nighantu: Related: Nighantu 1.1 — go listed among pṛthivī-vācaka and dhana-vācaka words. Gopa as compound.
meanings.nirukta: Compound: go (cattle/earth/rays) + pa (from √pā, to protect). 'Gāḥ pālayati iti gopaḥ' — one who protects cattle. In Vedic usage, gopāḥ can mean 'protectors' in general.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.9.56 — गोपालः listed as synonym: 'gopālaḥ gopaḥ' — cowherd terms.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 8: 'gopā adṛśan' — 'the cowherds saw [him]'. The cowherds represent ordinary people who perceive the divine in nature (the sun). This democratizes the vision — Rudra reveals himself not just to sages but to common herders. In Vaiṣṇava-Śaiva synthesis, the gopa theme connects to Kṛṣṇa-Gopāla.

dṛś.root: √दृश् (dṛś — to see, to behold, to perceive) — bhvādi/tudādi, parasmaipada

grammar.forms: अदृशन् (adṛśan, 3rd pl. imperfect 'they saw'), दृष्टः (dṛṣṭaḥ, past participle nom. sg. 'having been seen')
panini: P 3.2.111 — laṅ (imperfect): अ + दृश् + अन् → अदृशन् (Vedic form without guṇa)
P 3.2.102 — past participle: दृश् + क्त → दृष्ट (with ṣ per P 8.2.36)

meanings.english: To see; to behold; to perceive; to have the vision of
meanings.nirukta: √dṛś — 'paśyati darśayati' (to see, to show). In mantra 8: 'adṛśan' (they saw) is repeated — first by cowherds, then by water-bearers. The past participle 'dṛṣṭaḥ' means 'having been seen/perceived'.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.1.36 — √दृश् listed meaning 'paśyati' (to see). दृष्टिः (vision) in Amarakośa 2.6.54.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 8: 'sa dṛṣṭo mṛḍayāti naḥ' — 'he, having been seen, shows mercy to us'. The act of seeing (darśana) Rudra is itself salvific — his being perceived triggers his grace. This is the Vedāntic principle that the divine reveals itself (sākṣātkāra) and that revelation itself is liberating. Cf. Brahma Sūtra 1.1.2: 'janmādy asya yataḥ' — Brahman is known by its effects.

udahārya.root: उद (ud — up) + अहार्य (from उदक/अप् — water) — or उद + √हृ (to carry) with ya suffix — water-carriers; water-maidens

grammar.base: उदहार्य (udahārya — water-carriers, those who carry water)
grammar.forms: उदहार्यः (udahāryaḥ, nom. pl.)
panini: P 3.1.118 — ṇyat-pratyaya: udaka + √hṛ + ya → udahārya (one who should carry water / water-carrier)

meanings.english: Water-carriers; those who draw/carry water; water-bearers
meanings.nirukta: From udaka (water) + √hṛ (to carry). 'Udakam harantīti udahāryaḥ' — those who carry water. Sāyaṇa identifies them as women going to fetch water from rivers — ordinary people encountering the divine in daily life.
meanings.vedantic: The water-carriers, like the cowherds, represent the common people who see Rudra (the sun) in their daily activities. The divine is not hidden from the ordinary — Rudra reveals himself to all, not just to the ritually initiated.

grammar.forms: विश्वा (viśvā, neut. nom. pl. 'all'), विश्वतः (viśvataḥ, adverb 'from all sides/everywhere')
panini: P 1.1.27 — viśva classified as sarvanāman
P 5.3.7 — tasil-pratyaya: viśva + tas → viśvatas (from all sides)

meanings.english: All; entire; universal; every; the whole; from all sides (as viśvataḥ)
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.1 — listed among words denoting 'sarva' (totality/completeness).
meanings.nirukta: From √viś — 'viśati prāpnoti sarvam iti viśvam' (that which enters/pervades everything). Viśva denotes totality in an all-pervading sense.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.1.1 — विश्वम् listed as synonym of sarva: 'sarvaḥ viśvaḥ'.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 8: 'viśvā bhūtāni' — 'all beings'. The entire manifest creation sees Rudra-as-sun. Viśvataḥ (from all sides) in mantras 13 and 15 emphasizes comprehensive protection — Rudra's grace covers all directions.

bhūta.root: √भू (bhū — to be, to become) + त (kta — past participle suffix)

grammar.base: भूत (bhūta — being, creature, element; that which has come into being)
grammar.forms: भूतानि (bhūtāni, neut. nom. pl. 'beings/creatures')
panini: P 3.2.102 — kta-pratyaya: भू + त → भूत
P 7.1.20 — neut. nom. pl.: bhūtāni

meanings.english: Beings; creatures; living entities; elements; that which has come into existence
meanings.nighantu: Related: Nighantu 1.1 — bhūta related terms among prāṇi-vācaka (living-being-denoting) words.
meanings.nirukta: From √bhū — 'bhūtam = jātam prāptam' (that which has been born/come into being). Encompasses all living creatures.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.7 — bhūtam listed in dual sense: (1) prāṇī (living being), (2) mahābhūta (the five great elements: earth, water, fire, air, ether).
meanings.vedantic: In Vedānta, 'bhūta' refers to both living beings and the five elements (pañca-mahābhūta) from which the world is composed. 'Viśvā bhūtāni' — all beings/entities without exception perceive Rudra.

sahasrākṣa.root: सहस्र (sahasra — thousand) + अक्ष (akṣa — eye, from √अक्ष् — to see)

grammar.forms: सहस्राक्षाय (sahasrākṣāya, dat. sg.), सहस्राक्ष (sahasrākṣa, voc. sg.)
panini: P 2.2.24 — bahuvrīhi: sahasra + akṣa → sahasrākṣa (one who has a thousand eyes)
P 6.1.87 — savarna-dīrgha sandhi: a + a → ā: sahasra + akṣa → sahasrākṣa

meanings.english: Thousand-eyed; omniscient; all-seeing; one who sees everything
meanings.nighantu: Components: Nighantu 2.4 — akṣi/akṣa listed among netra-vācaka (eye-denoting) words.
meanings.nirukta: Bahuvrīhi: 'sahasram akṣāṇi yasya saḥ' — one who has a thousand eyes. 'Thousand' is Vedic idiom for 'countless/infinite'. The compound signifies omniscience — the ability to see all things simultaneously.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.42 — सहस्राक्षः listed as an epithet of Indra. In Śiva context, it indicates the omniscience shared by all supreme deities.
meanings.vedantic: The thousand eyes represent sarva-jñatva (omniscience) — one of the essential attributes of Brahman (Brahma Sūtra 1.1.2). In the Puruṣa Sūkta (RV 10.90.1): 'sahasrākṣaḥ sahasrapāt' — the cosmic Puruṣa has a thousand eyes and a thousand feet. Rudra as sahasrākṣa is identified with this cosmic Puruṣa.

mīḍhuṣa.root: √मिह् (mih — to rain, to sprinkle, to be bountiful, to shed) + kvasu suffix

grammar.base: मीढ्वस् (mīḍhvas — bountiful, generous, one who rains gifts)
grammar.forms: मीढुषे (mīḍhuṣe, dat. sg. 'to the bountiful one'), मीढुष्टम (mīḍhuṣṭama, superl. voc. 'O most bountiful')
panini: P 3.2.107 — kvasu-pratyaya (perfect participle): मिह् + क्वसु → मीढ्वस् (with cerebral ḍh per P 8.2.32)
P 5.3.55 — superlative: mīḍhuṣ + tama → mīḍhuṣṭama (most bountiful)

meanings.english: The bountiful one; the generous giver; one who rains down gifts; the munificent
meanings.nighantu: Related: Nighantu 2.7 — words for dāna (giving/generosity).
meanings.nirukta: From √mih — originally 'to rain/sprinkle'. Extended meaning: 'to shower gifts'. 'Mīḍhvān = dātā' — one who gives bountifully. The perfect participle form suggests one who has abundantly given — an established giver. Sāyaṇa: 'kāmānāṃ varṣitā' — one who rains desired things.
meanings.vedantic: Rudra as mīḍhuṣa transforms from the terrifying to the bountiful — the same deity who wields arrows also showers blessings. In mantra 13, the superlative 'mīḍhuṣṭama' (O most bountiful) is the climactic address — the peak of praising Rudra's generosity.

atho.root: अथ (atha — now, then, moreover) + उ (u — emphatic particle)

grammar.base: अथो (atho — and moreover, and further, and also)
panini: P 6.1.96 — sandhi: atha + u → atho (a + u → o)

meanings.english: And moreover; and further; and also; furthermore
meanings.nirukta: Compound particle: atha (now/then/moreover) + u (emphatic). Introduces an additional thought — 'and furthermore'. In mantra 9: 'atho ye asya satvānaḥ' — 'and moreover, those who are his attendants'.
meanings.vedantic: Atho transitions from praising Rudra directly to acknowledging his retinue (gaṇas/attendants). This expansion from the one to the many is a hallmark of Vedic theology.

satvān.root: सत्व (satva — being, existence, warrior) + possessive suffix, or from √सत् (sat — to be) with van suffix

grammar.base: सत्वन् (satvan — warrior, attendant, strong being, companion)
grammar.forms: सत्वानः (satvānaḥ, nom. pl. masc. 'the attendants/warriors')
panini: P 3.2.130 — vanip-pratyaya: sat + van → satvan (one who possesses being/strength)

meanings.english: Attendants; warriors; companions; strong beings; Rudra's retinue (gaṇas)
meanings.nirukta: From sat (being/warrior) + van (possessive). 'Sattvaṃ balam yasya' — one who possesses strength/being. Sāyaṇa glosses: 'anucarāḥ' (attendants, followers). Refers to Rudra's gaṇas — the host of beings that accompany the Lord.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 9: 'ye asya satvānaḥ' — 'those who are his attendants'. The devotee offers salutation not just to Rudra but to his entire retinue. In Śaiva theology, the gaṇas (attendants) represent the various forces and beings that serve the cosmic will of Śiva-Rudra.

panini: P 7.2.86 — nominative singular suppletion: asmad → aham

meanings.english: I (first person singular nominative)
meanings.nirukta: The emphatic first-person pronoun. In mantra 9: 'ahaṃ tebhyo'karaṃ namaḥ' — 'I made salutation to them'. The shift from plural 'we' (naḥ) to singular 'I' (aham) is significant — the individual devotee personally commits to honoring Rudra's attendants.
meanings.vedantic: In Vedānta, 'aham' is analyzed extensively — it is the locus of ahaṅkāra (ego) but also of the Ātman. 'Aham brahmāsmi' (Bṛhadāraṇyaka 1.4.10). Here the devotee uses 'aham' in the act of surrender — the 'I' that bows is simultaneously transcended by the act of bowing.

pra_muc.root: प्र (pra — forth, away) + √मुच् (muc — to release, to set free, to loosen)

grammar.base: प्र√मुच् (pramuc — to release, to loosen, to untie)
grammar.forms: प्रमुञ्च (pramuñca, 2nd sg. imperative 'release! loosen!')
panini: P 3.4.87 — loṭ (imperative): प्र + मुच् + हि → प्रमुञ्च (with nasal per P 7.1.59)

meanings.english: Release; loosen; set free; unstring (the bow)
meanings.nirukta: Compound verb: pra (forth/away) + √muc (to release). 'Pramuñca dhanvanaḥ jyām' — 'release/unstring the bowstring'. The imperative is a direct petition to Rudra to disarm.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.1.18 — √मुच् listed meaning 'mokṣaṇa' (releasing).
meanings.vedantic: The prayer to 'release the bowstring' asks Rudra to disarm — to set aside his destructive capacity. In Vedāntic terms, 'release' (mokṣa) is the ultimate prayer — pramuc contains the seed of 'pramukti' (liberation). Asking Rudra to release his weapon is simultaneously asking for our own release from bondage.

ārtnī.root: आ + √ṛt (to fasten) + ni — the tip/end of the bow where the string is fastened

grammar.base: आर्त्नि (ārtnī — the tip of the bow; the end where the bowstring is tied)
grammar.forms: आर्त्नियोः (ārtniyoḥ, gen./loc. dual 'of/at the two bow-tips')
panini: P 7.3.112 — dual case forms for i-stem: ārtnī + oḥ → ārtniyoḥ

meanings.english: The tip/end of the bow where the string is tied; the two extremities of the bow
meanings.nirukta: From ā + √ṛt (to fasten/fix) — 'ṛtyate yasyāṃ sā ārtnī' (that at which [the string] is fastened). The dual 'ubhayoḥ ārtniyoḥ' — from both tips of the bow. Sāyaṇa: 'dhanuṣaḥ ubhayoḥ koṭyoḥ' (both ends of the bow).
meanings.vedantic: The technical specificity — asking for the bowstring to be released from both tips — reflects the Vedic precision in prayer. Nothing is left to chance; the request is complete and specific.

jyā.root: √ज्या (jyā — to overpower, to overwhelm; also: the bowstring)

grammar.forms: ज्याम् (jyām, acc. sg. 'the bowstring')
panini: P 7.1.18 — ā-stem feminine: acc. sg. ज्याम्

meanings.english: Bowstring; string of the bow; that which overpowers
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 2.20 — grouped with āyudha-related terms.
