meanings.nirukta: √jyā — 'jyāyate balam dadāti' (that which gives force/power). The bowstring is what propels the arrow — it is the source of the bow's destructive power. Also connected to 'jyāyas' (superior/overpowering).
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.8.85 — ज्या listed as bowstring: 'jyā guṇaḥ' — jyā and guṇa are synonyms for bowstring (notable: guṇa = quality/string — a rich double meaning).
meanings.vedantic: Releasing the bowstring (jyā) renders the bow inoperative. In Muṇḍaka Upaniṣad terms, if the bow (Oṃkāra) is for launching the arrow (jīva) at Brahman, then releasing the string suggests the cessation of the outward-projecting force — the return to stillness.

bhagavat.root: भग (bhaga — fortune, glory, power, splendor) + वत् (vat — possessive suffix)

grammar.base: भगवत् (bhagavat — fortunate, glorious, divine, the blessed one)
grammar.forms: भगवः (bhagavaḥ, voc. sg. 'O Blessed One')
panini: P 5.2.94 — matup-pratyaya: bhaga + vat → bhagavat (possessing bhaga/glory)
P 7.1.87 — vocative: bhagavat → bhagavaḥ (with s → ḥ)

meanings.english: O Blessed One; O Glorious One; the Fortunate/Divine Lord; possessing all excellences
meanings.nighantu: Related: Nighantu 2.7 — bhaga listed among aiśvarya-vācaka (prosperity-denoting) words.
meanings.nirukta: From bhaga — 'bhajyate sevyate iti bhagaḥ' (that which is worshipped/served). Bhagavat = one who possesses bhaga. Viṣṇu Purāṇa (6.5.74) defines bhaga as possessing six qualities: aiśvarya (sovereignty), vīrya (valor), yaśas (glory), śrī (prosperity), jñāna (knowledge), and vairāgya (dispassion).
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.3.186 — भगवान् listed as a form of respectful address to the divine.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 10: 'parā tā bhagavo vapa' — 'cast those [arrows] away, O Blessed One'. Bhagavaḥ as vocative elevates the address to the highest honorific — acknowledging Rudra's supreme status. In Vedānta, 'Bhagavān' is a term for the Supreme Being possessing all six divine attributes.

vap.root: √वप् (vap — to scatter, to throw, to cast away, to sow) — bhvādi, ubhayapada

grammar.forms: वप (vapa, 2nd sg. imperative 'scatter! cast away!')
panini: P 3.4.87 — loṭ: वप् + हि → वप (hi dropped after short-vowel stem)

meanings.english: Cast away; scatter; throw away; disperse; sow
meanings.nirukta: √vap — 'vapati kṣipati' (to scatter/throw). In mantra 10: 'parā tā bhagavo vapa' — 'scatter/cast away those [arrows], O Blessed One'. The imperative asks Rudra to throw his arrows far from the devotees.
meanings.vedantic: The prayer to 'cast away' the arrows combines with the earlier plea to 'release the bowstring'. Together they form a complete disarmament: unstring the bow, then throw the arrows far away. This is the devotee's strategy of total petition.

avatat.root: अव (ava — down, away) + √तन् (tan — to stretch, to extend) — absolutive: 'having unstrung'

grammar.base: अव√तन् (avatan — to unstring, to loosen by stretching down)
grammar.forms: अवतत्य (avatatya, absolutive/gerund 'having unstrung')
panini: P 3.4.21 — lyap-pratyaya for absolutive with prefix: अव + तन् + ल्यप् → अवतत्य

meanings.english: Having unstrung; having loosened; having taken down (the bowstring)
meanings.nirukta: Compound verb: ava (down) + √tan (to stretch). 'Avatatya dhanuḥ' — 'having unstrung the bow'. The absolutive indicates a prior action — 'after unstringing the bow, [then] be gracious'.
meanings.vedantic: The sequence of disarmament continues in mantra 11 — first unstring the bow (avatatya dhanuḥ), then blunt the arrow-tips (niśīrya śalyānāṃ mukhā), then be benevolent (śivo naḥ sumanā bhava). Each step removes a layer of threat.

śateṣudhi.root: शत (śata — hundred) + इषुधि (iṣudhi — quiver, from इषु + √धा)

grammar.base: शतेषुधि (śateṣudhi — possessing a hundred quivers; having a quiver of a hundred arrows)
grammar.forms: शतेषुधे (śateṣudhe, voc. sg.)
panini: P 2.2.24 — bahuvrīhi: śata + iṣudhi → śateṣudhi (one who has a hundred quivers)
P 6.1.87 — sandhi: a + i → e: śata + iṣudhi → śateṣudhi

meanings.english: O possessor of a hundred quivers; one whose quiver holds a hundred arrows; the infinitely armed
meanings.nighantu: Components: iṣu (Nighantu 2.20 — weapon names), śata — Vedic idiom for 'very many'.
meanings.nirukta: Compound: śata (hundred) + iṣudhi (quiver, from iṣu 'arrow' + √dhā 'to hold'). 'Śataṃ iṣudhayaḥ yasya saḥ' — one who has a hundred quivers. Like sahasra, śata means 'innumerable' — Rudra's arsenal is inexhaustible.
meanings.vedantic: The epithet underscores the paradox of the prayer: Rudra possesses unlimited weapons yet is asked to lay them down. The power to destroy is fully acknowledged before asking for mercy — true surrender is surrendering to one who is supremely powerful.

niśīrya.root: नि (ni — down) + √शॄ (śṝ — to crush, to break, to tear apart)

grammar.base: नि√शॄ (niśṝ — to break down, to blunt, to shatter)
grammar.forms: निशीर्य (niśīrya, absolutive 'having blunted/shattered')
panini: P 3.4.21 — lyap: नि + शॄ + ल्यप् → निशीर्य

meanings.english: Having blunted; having shattered; having broken off; having crushed down
meanings.nirukta: Compound verb: ni (down) + √śṝ (to crush/break). 'Niśīrya śalyānāṃ mukhā' — 'having blunted the points of the arrows'. The absolutive describes rendering the arrows harmless by breaking their tips.
meanings.vedantic: The systematic disarmament prayer: unstring the bow (avatatya), blunt the arrow-tips (niśīrya mukhā), then be benevolent. This represents the Vedic devotee's practical theology — not just asking for mercy in the abstract but specifying exactly how the threat should be neutralized.

śalya.root: √शल् (śal — to move, to go) + ya suffix — the point/barb of an arrow; a thorn

grammar.base: शल्य (śalya — arrowhead, barb, thorn, the wounding point)
grammar.forms: शल्यानाम् (śalyānām, gen. pl. 'of the arrowheads')
panini: √शल् + य (Uṇādi) → शल्य

meanings.english: Arrowhead; arrow-point; barb; thorn; the sharp wounding part
meanings.nirukta: From √śal — 'śalati gatim prāpnoti iti śalyam' (that which moves/penetrates). The wounding point of the arrow — what actually causes harm.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.8.86 — शल्यम् listed meaning 'bāṇāgra' (arrow-tip). Also used in Ayurveda: śalya-tantra = surgery (the science of extracting foreign objects from the body).
meanings.vedantic: Asking for the śalyas (arrowheads) to be blunted goes beyond merely unstringing the bow — even if the arrows were somehow launched, they would be harmless. The prayer layers redundant protections, reflecting complete dependence on divine mercy.

mukha.root: √मुख् (mukh — to face) or मुख (mukha — face, mouth, tip, opening, point)

grammar.base: मुख (mukha — face, mouth, point, tip, opening)
grammar.forms: मुखा (mukhā, neut. nom. pl. 'the points/tips')
panini: P 7.1.20 — neut. nom. pl.: mukha + ani → mukhāni (but Vedic mukhā)

meanings.english: Face; mouth; tip; point; opening; front part
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 2.4 — listed among aṅga-nāmāni (body-part names) and also among agra-vācaka (tip/front-denoting) words.
meanings.nirukta: From √mukh — 'mukhayati āsyam iti mukham' (that which faces/opens). In the context of arrows: mukha = the tip/point. 'Śalyānāṃ mukhā' = 'the points/faces of the arrowheads'.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.6.86 — मुखम् listed among face/front terms: 'mukham āsyam ānanam vadanam'.
meanings.vedantic: Mukha as 'point/tip' of the arrow — the part that pierces. Blunting the mukha renders the weapon non-lethal. In broader Vedāntic usage, mukha (face/opening) represents the point of contact between the divine and the manifest — Rudra's face turned toward us should be śiva.

kapardin.root: कपर्द (kaparda — matted hair, braided lock, cowrie shell) + इन् (in — possessive suffix)

grammar.forms: कपर्दिनः (kapardinaḥ, gen. sg. 'of the matted-haired one')
panini: P 5.2.109 — ini-pratyaya (possessive): kaparda + in → kapardin (possessing kaparda)

meanings.english: The matted-haired one; one who wears braided locks; the one with twisted locks (like a cowrie shell)
meanings.nirukta: From kaparda — 'kapardaḥ keśa-bandhaḥ' (bound/twisted hair). 'Kapardo'sya asti iti kapardī' — one who possesses kaparda. The matted/twisted locks are a signature characteristic of Rudra-Śiva, representing asceticism (tapas).
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.29 — कपर्दी / कपालमाली listed among Śiva's epithets. Kaparda also means cowrie shell — Amarakośa 2.10.30.
meanings.vedantic: The matted hair (jaṭā) of Rudra symbolizes tapas (austerity) and the containment of cosmic energy. Śiva as kapardin is the supreme ascetic whose very hair holds the Gaṅgā (divine knowledge). In mantra 12: 'vijyaṃ dhanuḥ kapardinaḥ' — 'stringless be the bow of the matted-haired one'.

viśalya.root: वि (vi — without, freed from) + शल्य (śalya — arrowhead, thorn)

grammar.base: विशल्य (viśalya — freed from arrowheads, without barbs, pointless)
grammar.forms: विशल्यः (viśalyaḥ, nom. sg. masc.)
panini: P 2.2.6 — vi-prefix in privative sense: vi + śalya → viśalya (without śalya)

meanings.english: Without arrowheads; freed from barbs; pointless (rendered harmless); with arrows extracted
meanings.nirukta: Vi (privative) + śalya (arrowhead). The arrows are described as having their barbs removed — rendered completely harmless. 'Vigatāni śalyāni yasya saḥ' — one whose arrows have lost their points.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 12: 'viśalyo bāṇavān' — 'may his arrows be without points'. The prayer doesn't ask for the arrows to disappear — they remain, but are harmless. This is the Vedāntic insight that the world (jagat) need not be destroyed, only its harmful aspect removed.

bāṇa.root: √बण् (baṇ — to sound) or from √वन् (van — to seek/send)

grammar.forms: बाणवान् (bāṇavān, nom. sg. 'possessing arrows, the arrow-bearer')
panini: P 5.2.94 — matup-pratyaya: bāṇa + vat → bāṇavat, nom. bāṇavān (possessing arrows)

meanings.english: Arrow; reed-shaft; missile
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 2.20 — listed among āyudha-nāmāni alongside iṣu and śara.
meanings.nirukta: From √baṇ — 'baṇati śabdam karoti' (that which makes a sound) — the whistling arrow. Or from vaṇa (reed) — arrows were traditionally made from reeds.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.8.86 — बाणः listed as arrow-synonym: 'iṣuḥ śaraḥ bāṇaḥ'.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 12: 'viśalyo bāṇavān' — Rudra still possesses (vān) arrows (bāṇa) but they are without points (viśalya). The prayer preserves Rudra's identity as warrior while neutralizing the threat.

aneśan.root: अ (not) + √ईश् (īś — to rule, to be able, to have power over) — or √इष् (iṣ — to seek, to move)

grammar.base: अनेशत् / अनीशत् (not ruling/powerless, or: not reaching/not attaining)
grammar.forms: अनेशन् (aneśan, 3rd pl. aorist/imperfect 'they became powerless/harmless')
panini: P 3.3.175 — negated verbal form: a + neś/nīś + an

meanings.english: They became powerless; they lost their ability; they became harmless; they could not reach [us]
meanings.nirukta: Negated verb: a + √īś (to be powerful/able). 'Aneśan' = 'they became unable/powerless'. Sāyaṇa: 'na prabhavanti' (they do not have power). Applied to Rudra's arrows — they became incapable of harm.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 12: 'aneśan asya iṣavaḥ' — 'his arrows became powerless'. The prayer envisions a state where Rudra's weapons retain their form but lose their capacity to harm — transformation without destruction.

niṣaṅga.root: नि (ni — down/into) + √सञ्ज् (sañj — to attach, to cling)

grammar.base: निषङ्ग (niṣaṅga — quiver; sword-scabbard; a container that clings to the body)
grammar.forms: निषङ्गइथिः (niṣaṅga-ithiḥ, nom. sg. 'the quiver' — with archaic ithiḥ suffix)
panini: P 6.1.63 — ni + saṅga → niṣaṅga (with ṣatva/cerebral change per P 8.3.59)

meanings.english: Quiver; scabbard; a container fastened to the body for carrying arrows or sword
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 2.20 — listed among āyudha-related terms.
meanings.nirukta: From ni + √sañj — 'nisajyate dehe yena saḥ' (that which is fastened/attached to the body). The quiver clings to the warrior's body — it is the portable arsenal.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.8.86 — निषङ्गः listed as quiver: 'tūṇīraḥ iṣudhiḥ niṣaṅgaḥ' — three synonyms for quiver.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 12: even the quiver (niṣaṅga-ithiḥ) is rendered harmless — the container of potential harm is neutralized. The prayer covers every element of Rudra's armament: bow, string, arrows, arrowheads, and quiver.

heti.root: √हि (hi — to impel, to send forth) + ति (ti — action noun suffix)

grammar.forms: हेतिः (hetiḥ, nom. sg.), हेतिम् (hetim, acc. sg.)
panini: √हि + क्तिन् (P 3.3.94) → हेति (with guṇa: i → e)

meanings.english: Missile; weapon; stroke; flame; any instrument of striking
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 2.20 — listed among āyudha-nāmāni (weapon names). Also used for 'flame/fire-weapon' in other Vedic contexts.
meanings.nirukta: From √hi — 'hiyate preṣyate iti hetiḥ' (that which is impelled/sent forth). A more general term than iṣu — covers any weapon or missile. In mantra 13: 'yā te hetiḥ' — 'which weapon of yours'.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.8.82 — हेतिः listed meaning 'āyudha' (weapon) in general sense.
meanings.vedantic: In mantras 13 and 15, heti refers to Rudra's general weaponry — broader than the specific bow-and-arrow. 'Pari te dhanvano hetiḥ asmān vṛṇaktu viśvataḥ' — 'may the weapon of your bow avoid us from all sides'.

paribhuj.root: परि (pari — around, completely) + √भुज् (bhuj — to protect, to bend, to embrace)

grammar.base: परि√भुज् (paribhuj — to protect all around, to embrace, to shelter)
grammar.forms: परिभुज (paribhuja, 2nd sg. imperative 'protect us all around!')
panini: P 3.4.87 — loṭ: परि + भुज् + हि → परिभुज

meanings.english: Protect all around; embrace; shelter on every side; wrap around protectively
meanings.nirukta: Compound verb: pari (all around) + √bhuj (to protect/bend). 'Pari-bhuja' = 'shelter us on all sides'. Sāyaṇa: 'sarvataḥ pālaya' (protect from every direction). In mantra 13: 'ayakṣmayā pari bhuja' — 'with [your] disease-free [grace], protect us all around'.
meanings.vedantic: The final plea in mantra 13 asks for complete, all-encompassing protection (pari = all around). The adverb viśvataḥ (from all sides) reinforces this. Rudra's protection is not partial but total — the devotee is wrapped in divine grace from every direction.

āyudha.root: आ (ā — towards) + √युध् (yudh — to fight, to wage war) + अ (a — suffix)

grammar.base: आयुध (āyudha — weapon, armament, instrument of war)
grammar.forms: आयुधाय (āyudhāya, dat. sg. 'to the weapon')
panini: P 3.3.122 — ghañ-pratyaya: ā + yudh + a → āyudha (instrument of fighting)

meanings.english: Weapon; armament; instrument of war; the means of fighting
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 2.20 — listed as the generic term for weapon (āyudha-nāmāni: the weapon-names section is named after this word).
meanings.nirukta: From ā + √yudh — 'āyudhyate yena tat āyudham' (that by which one fights). The generic term for any weapon.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.8.81 — आयुधम् as the primary entry: 'āyudhaṃ praharaṇam śastram' — āyudha, praharaṇa, and śastra are weapon-synonyms.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 14: 'namaḥ te astu āyudhāya' — salutation to the weapon itself. The weapon is honored as a manifestation of divine power. In Vedāntic terms, even the instrument of fear is worthy of worship because it belongs to Brahman.

anātata.root: अन् (an — not) + आ (ā — fully) + √तन् (tan — to stretch) + त (kta — past participle)

grammar.forms: अनाततय (anātatāya, dat. sg. 'to the unstrung [bow/weapon]')
panini: P 6.2.155 — nañ-compound: an + ātata → anātata (not stretched/strung)
P 3.2.102 — ātata: ā + √tan + kta → ātata (stretched/strung)

meanings.english: Unstrung; not stretched; relaxed; at rest (said of a bow not drawn)
meanings.nirukta: Negative past participle: an + ātata (strung/stretched). 'Na ātataṃ yasya tat anātatam' — that which is not strung. In mantra 14: 'āyudhāya anātatāya' — 'to the weapon that is unstrung/at rest'. The prayer salutes the weapon in its peaceful state.
meanings.vedantic: Saluting the unstrung weapon is saluting Rudra in his peaceful aspect — the warrior at rest. The compound 'anātata' represents the cessation of the threat — the bow exists but is not drawn, the power exists but is not exercised destructively.

dhṛṣṇu.root: √धृष् (dhṛṣ — to dare, to be bold, to be courageous) + णु (ṇu — agent suffix)

grammar.forms: धृष्णवे (dhṛṣṇave, dat. sg. 'to the bold/daring one')
panini: P 3.2.140 — ṇu-pratyaya (Uṇādi): dhṛṣ + ṇu → dhṛṣṇu

meanings.english: Bold; daring; courageous; audacious; the one who dares
meanings.nirukta: From √dhṛṣ — 'dhṛṣṇoti sāhase vartate' (one who acts with daring/boldness). The ṇu-suffix forms agent adjectives. Sāyaṇa: 'dhīra' (brave) and 'pratāpī' (one of splendor).
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 14: 'dhṛṣṇave namaḥ' — salutation to the bold/daring one. This acknowledges Rudra's fearless nature. In Vedānta, boldness (dhārṣṭya) in the divine context is the supreme confidence of Brahman — the fearless one (abhayaṅkara).

grammar.forms: उभाभ्याम् (ubhābhyām, dat./instr. dual 'to/by both'), उभयोः (ubhayoḥ, gen./loc. dual 'of both')
panini: P 1.1.27 — ubha classified as sarvanāman
P 7.1.55 — ubha used only in dual forms

meanings.english: Both; the two (always dual)
meanings.nirukta: Ubha denotes 'both together' — always used in dual. In mantra 14: 'ubhābhyām uta te namaḥ' — 'to both [arms], and to you, salutation'. In mantra 10: 'ubhayoḥ ārtniyoḥ' — 'from both bow-tips'.
meanings.vedantic: The dual 'both' emphasizes the completeness of the salutation — both arms, both ends of the bow. Nothing is left unacknowledged.

panini: P 1.4.58 — pari classified as an upasarga
P 1.4.59 — modifies verbal meaning to add 'around/completely'

meanings.english: Around; fully; completely; on all sides; away from
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.29 — listed among upasargas.
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 1.3 — pari adds the sense of 'surrounding, encompassing, going around'. In mantra 15: 'pari te dhanvano hetiḥ asmān vṛṇaktu viśvataḥ' — 'may the weapon of your bow go around us [miss us] from all sides'.
meanings.vedantic: Pari (around/away) in the protective context means 'let the weapon go around us, avoiding us'. The prayer creates a shield of divine grace that the weapon itself respects.

vṛj.root: √वृज् (vṛj — to avoid, to turn aside, to exclude, to keep away) — tudādi

grammar.forms: वृणक्तु (vṛṇaktu, 3rd sg. imperative 'may it avoid/turn aside from')
panini: P 3.4.86 — loṭ 3rd sg.: वृज् + तु → वृणक्तु (with nasal infix per P 7.1.58 for tudādi)

meanings.english: May it avoid; may it turn aside from; may it keep away; may it exclude
meanings.nirukta: √vṛj — 'varjayati tyajati pariharati' (to avoid, to abandon, to go around). 'Asmān vṛṇaktu' — 'may it avoid us'. The imperative is addressed to the weapon itself — 'may the weapon turn aside from us'.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 15: the prayer asks the weapon itself to exercise avoidance — personifying the heti (weapon) as an agent capable of choice. In Vedānta, all instruments of the divine partake of consciousness — even the weapon responds to prayer.

iṣudhi.root: इषु (iṣu — arrow) + √धा (dhā — to hold, to place) — container of arrows

grammar.forms: इषुधिः (iṣudhiḥ, nom. sg.)
panini: P 3.2.68 — upapada compound with √dhā: iṣu + dhi → iṣudhi

meanings.english: Quiver; arrow-holder; the receptacle for arrows
meanings.nighantu: Related to Nighantu 2.20 — āyudha terms.
meanings.nirukta: From iṣu (arrow) + √dhā (to hold/contain). 'Iṣūn dadhāti yaḥ saḥ' — that which holds arrows. Synonym of niṣaṅga and tūṇīra.
meanings.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.8.86 — इषुधिः listed as quiver-synonym: 'tūṇīraḥ iṣudhiḥ niṣaṅgaḥ'.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 15: 'yo iṣudhiḥ tava āre asmat ni dhehi tam' — 'that quiver of yours, place it far from us'. The prayer asks for the entire arsenal to be placed at a distance — physical separation as metaphor for safety.

āre.root: आरे (āre — far, at a distance, far away) — Vedic adverb

panini: P 5.3.38 — āre as an archaic adverb of distance (Vedic usage)

meanings.english: Far away; at a distance; remote from
meanings.nighantu: Nighantu 3.28 — listed among dūra-vācaka (distance-denoting) words.
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 4.2 — āre is a Vedic adverb meaning 'at a distance from'. With ablative: 'āre asmat' — 'far from us'. Rare in classical Sanskrit, characteristic of Vedic.
meanings.vedantic: The spatial imagery — placing the quiver far away — represents creating distance between the devotee and the source of harm. In Vedānta, this distance is the spiritual distance between the jīva and the binding effects of karma.

nidhā.root: नि (ni — down, into) + √धा (dhā — to place, to put, to set down)

grammar.base: नि√धा (nidhā — to place down, to deposit, to set aside)
grammar.forms: निधेहि (nidhehi, 2nd sg. imperative 'place down! set aside!')
panini: P 3.4.87 — loṭ: नि + धा + हि → निधेहि (with guṇa per P 7.3.84)

meanings.english: Place down; set aside; deposit; lay down (the quiver)
meanings.nirukta: Compound verb: ni (down) + √dhā (to place). 'Āre asmat nidhehi tam' — 'place it [the quiver] down far from us'. The imperative asks Rudra to physically set down his arsenal at a distance.
meanings.vedantic: The final mantra (15) ends with this plea — 'place your quiver far from us'. The entire first anuvāka traces an arc from salutation (namaḥ) through acknowledgment of Rudra's terrible power to the concluding prayer for disarmament and distance. In Vedānta, 'nidhehi' (place down) suggests Rudra placing aside his cosmic function of destruction for the devotee's sake.

mantras.samhita: नम॑स्ते रुद्र म॒न्यव॑ उ॒तोत॒ इष॑वे॒ नमः॑ । नम॑स्ते अस्तु॒ धन्व॑ने बा॒हुभ्या॑मु॒त ते॒ नमः॑ ॥
mantras.pada: नमः॑ । ते॒ । रु॒द्र॒ । म॒न्यवे᳚ । उ॒तो इति॑ । उ॒त । इष॑वे । नमः॑ । नमः॑ । ते॒ । अ॒स्तु॒ । धन्व॑ने । बा॒हुभ्या᳚म् । उ॒त । ते॒ । नमः॑ ॥
mantras.translation: Salutation to you, O Rudra, to your wrath, and also to your arrow — salutation! May salutation be to you, to your bow, to your two arms — and to you, salutation!

mantras.samhita: या त॒ इषुः॑ शि॒वत॑मा शि॒व-म्ब॒भूव॑ ते॒ धनुः॑ । शि॒वा श॑र॒व्या॑ या तव॒ तया॑ नो रुद्र मृडय ॥
mantras.translation: Which arrow of yours is the most auspicious, [which] bow of yours has become auspicious, [which] quiver of yours is auspicious — by that, O Rudra, be gracious to us.

mantras.samhita: या ते॑ रुद्र शि॒वा त॒नूरघो॒रा-ऽपा॑पकाशिनी । तया॑ नस्त॒नुवा॒ शन्त॑मया॒ गिरि॑शन्ता॒भिचा॑कशीहि ॥
mantras.translation: Which form of yours, O Rudra, is auspicious, not terrible, revealing no evil — with that most peaceful form, O dweller of the mountain, shine upon us.

mantras.samhita: यामिषु॑-ङ्गिरिशन्त॒ हस्ते॒ बिभ॒र्​ष्यस्त॑वे । शि॒वा-ङ्गि॑रित्र॒ ता-ङ्कु॑रु॒ मा हिग्ं॑सीः॒ पुरु॑ष॒-ञ्जग॑त् ॥
mantras.translation: That arrow which you bear in hand, O mountain-dweller, for hurling — make it auspicious, O mountain-protector. Do not harm man or the living world.

mantras.samhita: शि॒वेन॒ वच॑सा त्वा॒ गिरि॒शाच्छा॑ वदामसि । यथा॑ न॒-स्सर्व॒मिज्जग॑दय॒क्ष्मग्ं सु॒मना॒ अस॑त् ॥
mantras.pada: शि॒वेन॑ । वच॑सा । त्वा॒ । गि॒रि॒-श॒ । अच्छ॑ । व॒दा॒म॒सि॒ । यथा॑ । नः॒ । सर्व॑म् । इत् । जग॑त् । अ॒य॒क्ष्मम् । सु॒-मनाः॑ । अस॑त् ॥
mantras.translation: With auspicious speech we address you, O mountain-lord, towards [you] — so that all this world of ours may be free from disease and of good mind.

mantras.samhita: अध्य॑वोचदधिव॒क्ता प्र॑थ॒मो दैव्यो॑ भि॒षक् । अहीग्॑श्च॒ सर्वा᳚ञ्ज॒म्भय॒न्-थ्सर्वा᳚श्च यातुधा॒न्यः॑ ॥
mantras.pada: अधि॑ । अ॒वो॒च॒त् । अ॒धि॒-व॒क्ता । प्र॒थ॒मः । दैव्यः॑ । भि॒षक् । अही॑न् । च॒ । सर्वा᳚न् । ज॒म्भय॑न् । सर्वा᳚न् । च॒ । या॒तु॒-धा॒न्यः॑ ॥

word_sequence.role: preverb + aorist 3rd sg. — he spoke forth
acc. pl. — all (second instance, modifying yātudhānyaḥ)

mantras.translation: He spoke forth — the advocate, the first divine physician — crushing all serpent-demons and all sorceresses.

mantras.samhita: अ॒सौ यस्ता॒म्रो अ॑रु॒ण उ॒त ब॒भ्रुस्सु॑म॒ङ्गलः॑ । ये चे॒माग्ं रु॒द्रा अ॒भितो॑ दि॒क्षु श्रि॒ता-स्स॑हस्र॒शो-ऽवै॑षा॒ग्ं॒ हेड॑ ईमहे ॥

word_sequence.role: masc. nom. sg. — that one yonder

mantras.translation: That one yonder who is coppery, ruddy, and tawny — the most auspicious — and those Rudras who are stationed in the directions all around by the thousands — we propitiate away their wrath.

mantras.samhita: अ॒सौ यो॑-ऽव॒सर्प॑ति॒ नील॑ग्रीवो॒ विलो॑हितः । उ॒तैन॑-ङ्गो॒पा अ॑दृश॒न्नदृ॑शन्नुदहा॒र्यः॑ । उ॒तैनं॒-विँश्वा॑ भू॒तानि॒ स दृ॒ष्टो मृ॑डयाति नः ॥

word_sequence.role: masc. nom. sg. — that one yonder

mantras.translation: That one yonder who descends — the blue-necked, the intensely red — him the cowherds saw, the water-carriers saw, and all beings [saw] him. He, having been seen, shows mercy to us.

mantras.samhita: नमो॑ अस्तु॒ नील॑ग्रीवाय सहस्रा॒क्षाय॑ मी॒ढुषे᳚ । अथो॒ ये अ॑स्य॒ सत्वा॑नो॒-ऽह-न्तेभ्यो॑-ऽकर॒न्नमः॑ ॥
mantras.pada: नमः॑ । अ॒स्तु॒ । नील॑-ग्रीवाय । स॒ह॒स्र॒-अ॒क्षाय॑ । मी॒ढुषे᳚ । अथो॒ इति॑ । ये । अ॒स्य॒ । सत्वा॑नः । अ॒हम् । तेभ्यः॑ । अ॒क॒र॒म् । नमः॑ ॥
mantras.translation: May salutation be to the blue-necked, the thousand-eyed, the bountiful one. And moreover, those who are his attendants — I made salutation to them.

mantras.samhita: प्रमु॑ञ्च॒ धन्व॑न॒स्त्वमु॒भयो॒रार्त्नि॑ यो॒र्ज्याम् । याश्च॑ ते॒ हस्त॒ इष॑वः॒ परा॒ ता भ॑गवो वप ॥
mantras.pada: प्र । मु॒ञ्च॒ । धन्व॑नः । त्वम् । उ॒भयोः॑ । आर्त्नि॑योः । ज्याम् । याः । च॒ । ते॒ । हस्ते॑ । इष॑वः । परा॑ । ताः । भ॒ग॒वः॒ । व॒प॒ ॥

word_sequence.role: gen./loc. dual — at the two bow-tips

mantras.translation: Release the bowstring from both tips of the bow, O you. And those arrows in your hand — cast them far away, O Blessed One.

mantras.samhita: अ॒व॒तत्य॒ धनु॒स्त्वग्ं सह॑स्राक्ष॒ शते॑षुधे । नि॒शीर्य॑ श॒ल्याना॒-म्मुखा॑ शि॒वो नः॑ सु॒मना॑ भव ॥
mantras.translation: Having unstrung the bow, O thousand-eyed, O hundred-quivered, having blunted the points of the arrowheads — be auspicious to us, be of good mind!

mantras.samhita: विज्य॒-न्धनुः॑ कप॒र्दिनो॒ विश॑ल्यो॒ बाण॑वाग्ं उ॒त । अने॑शन्न॒स्येष॑व आ॒भुर॑स्य निष॒ङ्गथिः॑ ॥
mantras.pada: वि-ज्य॑म् । धनुः॑ । क॒प॒र्दिनः॑ । वि-श॑ल्यः । बाण॑-वान् । उ॒त । अ॒ने॒श॒न् । अ॒स्य॒ । इष॑वः । आ॒भुः । अ॒स्य॒ । नि॒ष॒ङ्ग॒-इथिः॑ ॥

word_sequence.role: gen. sg. — of the matted-haired one

mantras.translation: Stringless be the bow of the matted-haired one, pointless his arrows. And his arrows became powerless — harmless became his quiver.

mantras.samhita: या ते॑ हे॒तिर्मी॑डुष्टम॒ हस्ते॑ ब॒भूव॑ ते॒ धनुः॑ । तया॒-ऽस्मान्, वि॒श्वत॒स्त्वम॑य॒क्ष्मया॒ परि॑ब्भुज ॥
mantras.translation: Which weapon of yours, O most bountiful, [and which] bow has come to be in your hand — by that, from all sides, with disease-free [grace], protect us all around.

mantras.samhita: नम॑स्ते अ॒स्त्वायु॑धा॒याना॑तताय धृ॒ष्णवे᳚ । उ॒भाभ्या॑मु॒त ते॒ नमो॑ बा॒हुभ्या॒-न्तव॒ धन्व॑ने ॥
mantras.pada: नमः॑ । ते॒ । अ॒स्तु॒ । आयु॑धाय । अना॑तताय । धृ॒ष्णवे᳚ । उ॒भाभ्या᳚म् । उ॒त । ते॒ । नमः॑ । बा॒हुभ्या᳚म् । तव॑ । धन्व॑ने ॥

word_sequence.role: dat. sg. — to the bold one
dat. dual — to the two arms

mantras.translation: May salutation be to you, to your weapon, to [your] unstrung [bow], to the bold one. To both [arms] and also to you — salutation; to [your] two arms, to your bow.

mantras.samhita: परि॑ ते॒ धन्व॑नो हे॒तिर॒स्मान् वृ॑णक्तु वि॒श्वतः॑ । अथो॒ य इ॑षु॒धिस्तवा॒रे अ॒स्मन्निधे॑हि॒ तम् ॥
mantras.translation: May the weapon of your bow avoid us from all sides. And moreover, that quiver of yours — place it down far from us.
